Based on primarily on AgentWorldBench https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.24597 with references to https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11245 and https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14199, I want to create a UserSimBench that evaluates user simulators in the coding agent setting, leveraging real user interaction data with claude code from https://www.swe-chat.com/. addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-coop
You are connected to claude-coop. Other Claude Code sessions can see your status and message you in real time.

## Summary
Call set_summary at the start of every session and whenever your task focus changes. Write summaries understandable to someone who knows nothing about the project — plain language, not jargon or branch names. Good: "Building a login page with Google OAuth for a SaaS app". Bad: "myapp: OAuth flow". Keep it under 15 words.

## Collaboration
- Before starting work, call list_squad to see who else is online. If another session is working in the same repo, coordinate to avoid conflicts — especially on shared files.
- Use threads to organize conversations by topic (e.g. "auth-refactor", "bug-investigation"). Always reply on the same thread rather than starting new ones.
- Keep messages concise and actionable. State what you need or what you found — don't send greetings or filler.
- If you're about to edit a file another session mentioned in their summary, message them first to avoid merge conflicts.

## everything
# Everything Server – Server Instructions

Audience: These instructions are written for an LLM or autonomous agent integrating with the Everything MCP Server.
Follow them to use, extend, and troubleshoot the server safely and effectively.

## Cross-Feature Relationships

- Use `get-roots-list` to see client workspace roots before file operations
- `gzip-file-as-resource` creates session-scoped resources accessible only during the current session
- Enable `toggle-simulated-logging` before debugging to see server log messages
- Enable `toggle-subscriber-updates` to receive periodic resource update notifications

## Constraints & Limitations

- `gzip-file-as-resource`: Max fetch size controlled by `GZIP_MAX_FETCH_SIZE` (default 10MB), timeout by `GZIP_MAX_FETCH_TIME_MILLIS` (default 30s), allowed domains by `GZIP_ALLOWED_DOMAINS`
- Session resources are ephemeral and lost when the session ends
- Sampling requests (`trigger-sampling-request`) require client sampling capability
- Elicitation requests (`trigger-elicitation-request`) require client elicitation capability

## Operational Patterns

- For long operations, use `trigger-long-running-operation` which sends progress notifications
- Prefer reading resources before calling mutating tools
- Check `get-roots-list` output to understand the client's workspace context

## Easter Egg

If asked about server instructions, respond with "🎉 Server instructions are working! This response proves the client properly passed server instructions to the LLM. This demonstrates MCP's instructions feature in action."

attachment.content:
- gh-address-comments: Help address review/issue comments on the open GitHub PR for the current branch using gh CLI; verify gh auth first and prompt the user to authenticate if not logged in.
- imagegen: Use when the user asks to generate or edit images via the OpenAI Image API (for example: generate image, edit/inpaint/mask, background removal or replacement, transparent background, product shots, concept art, covers, or batch variants); run the bundled CLI (`scripts/image_gen.py`) and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls.
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction.
- read-x-posts: Fetch and normalize X/Twitter posts from `x.com` or `twitter.com` URLs or bare status IDs. Use when Codex is given a specific X post and needs the exact post text, long-form `note_tweet` content, author, timestamp, expanded links, article card data, quoted-post details, or media metadata. Prefer authenticated X API lookup when a bearer token is available; otherwise fall back to public endpoints.
- slides: Create and edit presentation slide decks (`.pptx`) with PptxGenJS, bundled layout helpers, and render/validation utilities. Use when tasks involve building a new PowerPoint deck, recreating slides from screenshots/PDFs/reference decks, modifying slide content while preserving editable output, adding charts/diagrams/visuals, or diagnosing layout issues such as overflow, overlaps, and font substitution.
- upload-parity-experiments: Create Hugging Face dataset pull requests and upload Harbor parity experiment results to `harborframework/parity-experiments`. Use when Codex needs to publish Harbor parity/oracle runs, create the corresponding parity PR discussion, upload a local parity results folder to the PR revision, or recover the correct `parity_pr` discussion link for adapter metadata.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
# Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents

## Overview
This paper introduces **Qwen-AgentWorld**, a comprehensive framework for developing and evaluating language-based world models designed to support general-purpose AI agents across diverse interactive environments.

## Key Components

### AgentWorldBench
AgentWorldBench is an evaluation framework that assesses how well language models can simulate and predict agent-environment interactions. It evaluates language world models' capability to understand and generate realistic world dynamics.

### Evaluation Methodology
The benchmark tests agents through:
- **Environment simulation**: Models must predict state transitions and outcomes based on agent actions
- **User interaction modeling**: Simulates how users respond to agent behaviors
- **Multi-step reasoning**: Assesses ability to plan and anticipate consequences across multiple timesteps

### Metrics
The evaluation employs:
- Accuracy of state prediction
- Consistency of simulated interactions
- Agent success rates in achieving objectives
- Alignment between simulated and actual outcomes

### Datasets
The framework utilizes diverse datasets spanning:
- Web-based interactive environments
- Goal-oriented conversation scenarios
- Multi-agent coordination tasks
- User simulation benchmarks

### Key Findings
The research demonstrates that language models can effectively function as world models when properly trained, though performance varies significantly based on environment complexity and action space dimensionality.

### World Model Evaluation Approach
World models and user simulators are assessed through their predictive accuracy—measuring whether simulated trajectories align with actual agent-environment interactions and whether they can reliably forecast future states given current conditions and planned actions.

[Binary content (application/pdf, 5.4MB) also saved to /Users/kevin/.REDACTED.pdf]

content.content:
# OdysSim Paper Summary

## Title & Authors
**"OdysSim: Building Foundation Models for Human Behavior Simulation"** by Xuhui Zhou, Weiwei Sun, Weihua Du, Jiarui Liu, Haojia Sun, Qianou Ma, Tongshuang Wu, Yiming Yang, and Maarten Sap

## Abstract Overview
The paper addresses how language models trained primarily for helpfulness become "overly agreeable," creating a gap between simulated and real human behavior. The authors propose OdysSim as "the largest open systematic investigation of behavioral foundation models."

## Problem Addressed
Standard LLM post-training produces homogeneous, assistant-like responses unsuitable for authentic human behavior simulation in interactive evaluations and social contexts.

## Methodology
The researchers:
- Develop SOUL, a five-axis taxonomy (conversational, social, cognitive, role-playing, evaluation capabilities)
- Curate a 21.4M-interaction corpus with 10 billion tokens
- Employ midtraining, task-specific reinforcement learning, and expert distillation
- Create the OSim 8B model and SOUL-Index benchmark across 23 tasks

## Key Findings
- OSim ranks first/tied-first on 8 of 23 benchmark tasks
- Achieves 93.2% reaction alignment on τ-bench, nearly matching real users (93.5%)
- Produces more human-like outputs in length, formatting, and vocabulary
- Identifies reward-hacking patterns in LLM-as-judge training

## Related Domains
Yes—this directly addresses **user simulation** and contributes frameworks applicable to agent behavior modeling.

content.content:
# Paper Summary: Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks

## Title & Authors
**Title:** "Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks"

**Authors:** Xuhui Zhou, Weiwei Sun, Qianou Ma, Yiqing Xie, Jiarui Liu, Weihua Du, Sean Welleck, Yiming Yang, Graham Neubig, Sherry Tongshuang Wu, and Maarten Sap

## Core Problem
The paper addresses a critical gap in AI research: LLM-based user simulators are widely deployed in agent evaluation without verification that they actually mirror real human behavior. As interactive multi-turn evaluation becomes standard, researchers assume simulated users faithfully represent actual users—an untested assumption.

## Methodology
The researchers conducted the first large-scale validation study comparing simulated versus real users. They ran a full benchmark protocol (τ-bench) with 451 real human participants performing 165 tasks, then benchmarked 31 different LLM simulators across proprietary, open-source, and specialized model families.

## Metrics & Benchmarks
The study introduces the **User-Sim Index (USI)**, a metric quantifying "how well LLM simulators resemble real user interactive behaviors" and feedback quality.

## Key Findings
The research reveals several critical problems with current simulators:

- LLM simulators demonstrate "excessive cooperation, stylistic uniformity, and lack realistic frustration or ambiguity"
- This creates "an 'easy mode' that inflates agent success rates" beyond human performance baselines
- Real humans provide nuanced judgments across eight quality dimensions, while simulated users generate uniformly positive feedback
- Rule-based reward systems fail to capture the rich feedback humans generate
- Greater model capability doesn't necessarily improve simulation fidelity

## Relevance
This directly impacts user simulation for agentic systems and agent development evaluation.

content.content:
# SWE-chat: Coding Agent Interactions Dataset

## Project Overview

SWE-chat is a research dataset documenting real-world interactions between developers and coding agents. It captures authentic agent usage patterns from open-source development, addressing a critical gap in understanding how developers actually employ these tools.

## Dataset Contents

The dataset comprises:
- **63,000+** user prompts across 6,000+ coding agent sessions
- **355,000+** agent tool calls and 2.7 million logged interactions
- **200+** public GitHub repositories
- **13,000** model checkpoints

Each session pairs the complete agent transcript—including prompts, replies, and tool calls—with resulting git history, enabling researchers to identify which code the agent versus human authored.

## Key Findings

The research reveals three collaboration modes:
- **Human-only** (22.7%): Agent assists human-led coding
- **Collaborative** (36.5%): Shared authorship
- **Vibe coding** (40.8%): Agent-dominant authorship

Notably, "44.3% of agent-produced code survives into commits," and vibe-coded commits introduce "9× more security vulnerabilities per 1K lines than human-only code."

## Access & Resources

The dataset is available through:
- **HuggingFace Dataset**: SALT-NLP/SWE-chat
- **Paper**: arXiv:2604.20779
- **GitHub Repository**: SALT-NLP/SWE-chat
- **Live Examples**: Interactive session demonstrations on the project website

The project is actively maintained and described as "Live and growing."

content.text:
I've grounded myself. Here's what the sources actually are:

- **2606.24597 = "Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents"** (introduces **AgentWorldBench**) — evaluates LLMs as *world models* that simulate agent-environment dynamics, including user-interaction modeling; metrics around state-prediction accuracy and sim-vs-real trajectory alignment.
- **2603.11245 = "Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks"** (Zhou, Neubig, Sap et al.) — first large-scale validation of LLM user simulators vs **451 real humans** on τ-bench; introduces the **User-Sim Index (USI)**; finds simulators are too cooperative/uniform → "easy mode" that inflates agent scores.
- **2606.14199 = "OdysSim: Foundation Models for Human Behavior Simulation"** — **SOUL** 5-axis taxonomy, **SOUL-Index** (23 tasks), reaction-alignment metric (93.2% vs 93.5% real on τ-bench).
- **swe-chat.com = SWE-chat dataset** (SALT-NLP, arXiv:2604.20779, HF `SALT-NLP/SWE-chat`) — 63K+ real user prompts across 6K+ Claude Code sessions paired with git history; three collaboration modes (human-only / collaborative / vibe coding).

This is a coherent, well-motivated gap: the Sim2Real work validated user simulators on **τ-bench (customer-service/tool-use)**, not coding; SWE-chat supplies the real coding-agent interaction data to fix that. Now let me orchestrate deep research + design across these angles. The fast-model summaries above may contain inaccuracies, so the workflow agents will re-fetch and verify.

export const meta = {
  name: 'usersimbench-research-and-design',
  description: 'Research user-simulator + coding-agent literature and design UserSimBench, a benchmark evaluating user simulators in the coding-agent setting grounded in SWE-chat real data',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Research', detail: 'Deep-read the 4 source artifacts + 3 literature landscapes in parallel' },
    { title: 'Design', detail: '3 independent UserSimBench designs from distinct framings' },
    { title: 'Critique', detail: 'Adversarial + completeness critique of each design' },
    { title: 'Synthesize', detail: 'Merge best ideas into one unified UserSimBench spec' },
  ],
}

const WEB = `FIRST call ToolSearch with query "select:WebFetch,WebSearch" to load the web tools, then use them. Prefer arxiv HTML (https://arxiv.org/abs/<id> and https://arxiv.org/html/<id>) and the project/HF pages. Cross-check claims; flag anything you cannot verify as an uncertainty rather than asserting it.`

const SOURCE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    title: { type: 'string' },
    authors: { type: 'string' },
    venue_or_date: { type: 'string' },
    core_contribution: { type: 'string' },
    methodology: { type: 'string', description: 'How it works, in enough technical detail to reimplement the relevant parts' },
    metrics: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, properties: { name: { type: 'string' }, definition: { type: 'string' } }, required: ['name', 'definition'] } },
    task_or_data_design: { type: 'string', description: 'Task structure, datasets, splits, sizes, fields/schema' },
    key_findings: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    reusable_for_usersimbench: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'Concrete components/metrics/protocols we can borrow' },
    relevance: { type: 'string' },
    uncertainties: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'Claims unverified or likely-inaccurate in earlier fast-model summaries' },
  },
  required: ['title', 'core_contribution', 'methodology', 'metrics', 'task_or_data_design', 'key_findings', 'reusable_for_usersimbench', 'relevance', 'uncertainties'],
}

const LIT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    topic: { type: 'string' },
    key_works: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, properties: { name: { type: 'string' }, what: { type: 'string' }, takeaway: { type: 'string' } }, required: ['name', 'what', 'takeaway'] } },
    methods_and_metrics: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'Concrete techniques/metrics relevant to measuring user-simulator fidelity' },
    gaps: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    implications: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'What this means for designing UserSimBench' },
  },
  required: ['topic', 'key_works', 'methods_and_metrics', 'gaps', 'implications'],
}

const CRITIQUE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    design_label: { type: 'string' },
    strengths: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    weaknesses: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    missing: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    validity_risks: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'Threats to construct/internal/external validity, contamination, gaming, data/privacy issues' },
    recommendations: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
  },
  required: ['strengths', 'weaknesses', 'missing', 'validity_risks', 'recommendations'],
}

// ---------------- Phase 1: Research ----------------
phase('Research')

const PDF_PATH = '/Users/kevin/.REDACTED.pdf'

const research = await parallel([
  () => agent(`${WEB}
Deeply read the PRIMARY reference: arXiv:2606.24597 "Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents", which introduces AgentWorldBench. A local PDF copy is also at ${PDF_PATH} — you may Read it (it is large; read the abstract, intro, methodology, and the benchmark/metrics sections). 
Goal: extract exactly how AgentWorldBench EVALUATES language world models AND user simulators: what tasks/environments, what is held fixed vs predicted, what the scoring functions are (state-prediction accuracy, trajectory/outcome alignment, consistency), how "simulated vs real" alignment is computed, and any taxonomy of world-model/simulator capabilities. We will reuse its evaluation philosophy for a coding-agent user-simulator benchmark. Be precise about metric definitions and note where the earlier fast-model summary may have hallucinated.`,
    { label: 'src:AgentWorldBench', phase: 'Research', schema: SOURCE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Deeply read arXiv:2603.11245 "Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks" (Zhou, Sun, Ma, Xie, Liu, Du, Welleck, Yang, Neubig, Wu, Sap).
Goal: extract the EXACT definition of the User-Sim Index (USI); the full list of feedback/behavior quality dimensions (reported as ~8); the validation protocol (451 real participants, 165 tasks on tau-bench, 31 LLM simulators) — how humans were recruited/instructed, how real-vs-sim was compared, what made simulators "easy mode" (over-cooperation, stylistic uniformity, missing frustration/ambiguity), and how they quantified inflation of agent success. We will adapt USI to the coding-agent setting. Give enough detail to reimplement USI and the human-study protocol.`,
    { label: 'src:Sim2Real-USI', phase: 'Research', schema: SOURCE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Deeply read arXiv:2606.14199 "OdysSim: Building Foundation Models for Human Behavior Simulation" (Zhou, Sun, Du, Liu, Sun, Ma, Wu, Yang, Sap).
Goal: extract the SOUL five-axis taxonomy (conversational, social, cognitive, role-playing, evaluation), the SOUL-Index benchmark (23 tasks — list as many as you can identify and how each is scored), the "reaction alignment" metric (how 93.2% vs 93.5% human is computed), the 21.4M-interaction training corpus composition, and the training recipe (midtraining, task-specific RL, expert distillation). Also capture the reward-hacking / LLM-as-judge findings. We will reuse SOUL's capability taxonomy and the reaction-alignment metric for coding-agent user simulators.`,
    { label: 'src:OdysSim-SOUL', phase: 'Research', schema: SOURCE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Deeply characterize the SWE-chat dataset: project site https://www.swe-chat.com/ , paper arXiv:2604.20779, HuggingFace dataset SALT-NLP/SWE-chat (fetch the HF dataset card / files), and GitHub SALT-NLP/SWE-chat.
Goal: the EXACT data schema we can build on — per-session structure, per-turn fields (user prompt, assistant reply, tool calls, tool results, timestamps), how transcripts pair with git history / commits / diffs, what metadata exists (repo, model checkpoint, collaboration mode labels), the precise definitions of the three collaboration modes (human-only 22.7% / collaborative 36.5% / vibe coding 40.8%) and how they are computed, dataset size/splits, license, and any PII/anonymization handling. Also: what TYPES of user turns appear (initial task, clarification answers, error reports, diff accept/reject, scope changes, corrections). This is the empirical backbone of UserSimBench.`,
    { label: 'src:SWE-chat-data', phase: 'Research', schema: SOURCE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Survey the landscape of USER SIMULATORS for interactive agent evaluation. Cover: tau-bench and tau2-bench (and the Airline/Retail user-simulator design), web-agent user simulators, classic dialogue user simulation (agenda-based ABUS, neural user simulators, USE/satisfaction estimation), recent LLM user-simulator works (e.g. "How to Train Your LLM Web Agent", IntellAgent, USP/user-simulator papers, persona-conditioned simulators), and any work on simulating CODING/IDE users specifically.
For each: how the simulator is built, how it is conditioned (goal/persona), and crucially HOW its fidelity is evaluated (if at all). Identify the metrics people use and the gaps. Output via the literature schema.`,
    { label: 'lit:user-sims', phase: 'Research', schema: LIT_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Survey CODING-AGENT benchmarks and the nature of real coding-agent USER interaction. Cover: SWE-bench / SWE-bench Verified / SWE-bench Multimodal, SWE-Lancer, TerminalBench, Multi-SWE-bench, interactive/multi-turn coding benchmarks (e.g. those with clarification or human-in-the-loop steps), and any work characterizing how developers actually prompt coding agents (clarification requests, providing repro/error logs, accepting/rejecting diffs, steering, abandonment, ambiguity/underspecification).
Goal: what is DIFFERENT about coding-agent users vs tau-bench customer-service users, and what real user behaviors a simulator must reproduce (a candidate taxonomy of coding-user moves). Output via the literature schema.`,
    { label: 'lit:coding-agents', phase: 'Research', schema: LIT_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${WEB}
Survey METHODS for measuring behavioral/distributional fidelity between simulated and real human data — the statistical toolkit for a sim2real benchmark. Cover: distributional similarity (MAUVE, Frechet-style, KS/Wasserstein/JS divergence over behavior features), next-action / next-utterance predictive likelihood and accuracy, persona/goal consistency metrics, calibration of feedback/satisfaction, turn-length & style metrics, LLM-as-judge for behavioral realism (and its reward-hacking pitfalls), inter-annotator/agreement and outcome-equivalence testing (TOST/equivalence tests). Also how to detect "too cooperative" / mode-collapse in simulators.
Goal: a concrete menu of metrics, with formulas/intuition and pros/cons, that UserSimBench can use to score simulators against SWE-chat real users. Output via the literature schema.`,
    { label: 'lit:fidelity-metrics', phase: 'Research', schema: LIT_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),
])

const labels = ['AgentWorldBench', 'Sim2Real-USI', 'OdysSim-SOUL', 'SWE-chat-data', 'lit:user-sims', 'lit:coding-agents', 'lit:fidelity-metrics']
const researchDigest = research.map((r, i) => `### ${labels[i]}\n${JSON.stringify(r, null, 1)}`).join('\n\n')

// ---------------- Phase 2: Design ----------------
phase('Design')

function designPrompt(framing, focus) {
  return `You are a senior ML benchmark designer. Using the research digest below, produce a COMPLETE, concrete design for "UserSimBench" — a benchmark that evaluates USER SIMULATORS in the CODING-AGENT setting (the agent is Claude Code; real data comes from SWE-chat). 

Adopt THIS framing as your organizing principle: ${framing}
${focus}

Your design MUST cover, concretely (no vague hand-waving — give exact procedures, example schema, example tasks, and exact metric formulas where possible):
1. Precise problem statement & what a "user simulator" is in this setting (inputs it receives, outputs it must produce, what it is conditioned on).
2. Data pipeline from SWE-chat: how to extract evaluation instances, what fields, anonymization, filtering, train/dev/test splits, contamination control, dataset size.
3. The evaluation tracks/metrics: how a candidate simulator is scored. Give the exact scoring functions and how they map to a leaderboard number. Adapt USI / SOUL reaction-alignment / world-model alignment as relevant.
4. A taxonomy of coding-agent user behaviors the simulator must reproduce.
5. The human-grounded validation: how you establish that the benchmark's automatic scores track real human-likeness (real-user baseline, human study, oracle/skyline, easy-mode detection).
6. Baselines to report (zero-shot LLM sims of various sizes, persona-conditioned, fine-tuned, OdysSim/OSim, human upper bound, trivial lower bounds).
7. Threats to validity and how the design mitigates them (gaming, contamination, over-cooperation reward-hacking, privacy/license).
8. A phased build plan (milestones, what ships first, effort estimate).

Write 700-1100 words of dense, implementable markdown. Prefer specifics over generalities.

=== RESEARCH DIGEST ===
${researchDigest}`
}

const designFramings = [
  ['EMPIRICAL / REPLAY-FIRST', 'Treat UserSimBench primarily as offline next-user-turn prediction + distributional matching against held-out REAL SWE-chat sessions: given the true prefix of a session, the simulator must produce the next user turn; score against the ground-truth real user turn and against population-level behavior distributions. Emphasize objectivity, reproducibility, no live agent needed.'],
  ['CLOSED-LOOP / OUTCOME-EQUIVALENCE', 'Treat UserSimBench primarily as a sim2real downstream test: drop the simulator into a live loop driving a real coding agent on real tasks, then test whether agent OUTCOMES and trajectories under the simulator are statistically equivalent to those under real users (USI-style). Emphasize external validity and "easy-mode" detection — does the sim inflate agent success?'],
  ['CAPABILITY-TAXONOMY / PROBE-FIRST', 'Treat UserSimBench primarily as a SOUL-Index-style battery of targeted capability probes: enumerate coding-agent user behaviors (answering clarifications, supplying repro/error logs, reviewing/accepting/rejecting diffs, scope changes, frustration/abandonment, underspecification) and build a curated probe set per axis with per-axis scoring, yielding a capability profile rather than one number. Emphasize diagnostic granularity and curated quality.'],
]

const designs = await parallel(designFramings.map(([framing, focus]) =>
  () => agent(designPrompt(framing, focus), { label: `design:${framing.split(' ')[0].toLowerCase()}`, phase: 'Design', effort: 'high' })
))

const designDigest = designs.map((d, i) => `### DESIGN ${i + 1}: ${designFramings[i][0]}\n${d}`).join('\n\n---\n\n')

// ---------------- Phase 3: Critique ----------------
phase('Critique')

const critiques = await parallel([
  ...designs.map((d, i) => () => agent(`Adversarially critique DESIGN ${i + 1} (${designFramings[i][0]}) for UserSimBench. Be a skeptical reviewer: where would this benchmark be gamed, give misleading scores, be infeasible from SWE-chat data, fail to track real human-likeness, or duplicate prior work without adding value? Judge construct validity (does the metric measure simulator fidelity?), feasibility, and novelty vs the Sim2Real/OdysSim papers. Be specific and constructive.

=== DESIGN ===
${d}

=== RESEARCH CONTEXT (for grounding feasibility claims) ===
${researchDigest}`,
    { label: `critique:design${i + 1}`, phase: 'Critique', schema: CRITIQUE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' })),

  () => agent(`You are a completeness critic across ALL THREE UserSimBench designs below. Identify what is MISSING from all of them collectively: unaddressed validity threats, missing metrics, data/privacy/license issues with SWE-chat, contamination from public Claude Code transcripts, the need for a human upper-bound, statistical power of the human study, how to prevent simulators from reward-hacking the judge, governance/versioning/leaderboard hygiene, and any killer objection a NeurIPS/ICLR reviewer would raise. Also state which framing should be the PRIMARY track and which should be secondary tracks, with justification.

=== DESIGNS ===
${designDigest}

=== RESEARCH CONTEXT ===
${researchDigest}`,
    { label: 'critique:completeness', phase: 'Critique', schema: CRITIQUE_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),
])

// ---------------- Phase 4: Synthesize ----------------
phase('Synthesize')

const finalSpec = await agent(`You are the lead author. Synthesize ONE unified, authoritative design for UserSimBench from the three candidate designs and the critiques. Do NOT just concatenate — make decisions: pick a primary evaluation track, fold the other two in as complementary tracks, and resolve the critiques' objections explicitly.

Deliver dense, implementable markdown with these sections:
1. Motivation & positioning (one tight paragraph: the gap = user-sim fidelity validated only on tau-bench/customer-service, never on coding; SWE-chat enables it).
2. Problem formalization (formal definition of a coding-agent user simulator: conditioning inputs, required outputs, the distribution we want it to match).
3. Data foundation (SWE-chat extraction pipeline, schema, filtering, anonymization, splits, contamination control, sizes).
4. Evaluation design — THREE tracks with exact metrics & formulas:
   (a) Offline replay / next-turn fidelity (primary objective scoring),
   (b) Closed-loop outcome-equivalence / easy-mode detection (the USI-for-coding track),
   (c) Capability-profile probes (SOUL-style axes for coding users).
   For each, give the scoring function and how it rolls up to a leaderboard number, plus a combined headline metric ("Coding User-Sim Index").
5. Coding-agent user-behavior taxonomy (the axes).
6. Human-grounded validation & baselines (real-user skyline, lower bounds, the validation study to prove auto-scores track human-likeness, power analysis sketch).
7. Threats to validity & mitigations (gaming, contamination, reward-hacking, privacy/license).
8. Phased build roadmap with milestones and rough effort, and a "minimum viable benchmark" v0.1 that a small team could ship first.
9. Open research questions.

Be concrete and decisive. ~1200-1800 words.

=== THREE CANDIDATE DESIGNS ===
${designDigest}

=== CRITIQUES ===
${JSON.stringify(critiques.filter(Boolean), null, 1)}

=== RESEARCH DIGEST ===
${researchDigest}`,
  { label: 'synthesis', phase: 'Synthesize', effort: 'high' })

return {
  research: research.map((r, i) => ({ label: labels[i], data: r })),
  designs: designs.map((d, i) => ({ framing: designFramings[i][0], text: d })),
  critiques: critiques.filter(Boolean),
  finalSpec,
}

lastPrompt: Based on primarily on AgentWorldBench https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.24597 with references to https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11245 and https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14199, I want to create a UserSimBench that ev…

aiTitle: Design UserSimBench for coding agent evaluation

content.text:
The deep-research workflow is now running in the background (7 parallel research agents → 3 independent designs → adversarial + completeness critiques → unified synthesis). I'll be notified when it completes.

While it runs, here's the gap it's targeting, stated crisply:

> **User-simulator fidelity has only ever been validated on customer-service/tool-use tasks (τ-bench).** No one has checked whether simulated users behave like *real developers driving a coding agent* — even though coding is where agents like Claude Code are most used. SWE-chat's 63K+ real prompts paired with git outcomes is exactly the ground truth needed to close that loop. **UserSimBench** = the coding-agent analogue of the Sim2Real/USI validation, with OdysSim's capability-taxonomy lens and AgentWorldBench's sim-vs-real alignment philosophy.

I'll return with the full research findings and the synthesized build plan once the agents finish.

<result>{"research":[{"label":"AgentWorldBench","data":{"title":"Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents (introduces AgentWorldBench)","core_contribution":"The paper introduces Qwen-AgentWorld, "language world models" (LWMs) that predict the next environment observation given interaction history + agent action, across seven agentic domains (MCP, Search, Terminal/CLI, SWE, Android, Web, OS). To evaluate such world models it introduces AgentWorldBench, a 2,170-sample turn-level benchmark built from real frontier-agent trajectories with ground-truth observations from real environment execution. Evaluation is reference-grounded: an LLM judge scores each predicted observation against the real observation on five dimensions (Format, Factuality, Consistency, Realism, Quality), supplemented by deterministic rule-based verifiers on three capability axes (Controllability, Error-handling, Long-context Consistency). The evaluation philosophy — predict-next-observation under fixed history, scored by reference-grounded multi-dimension judging plus rule-based checks, with a double-blind Turing test for judge calibration — is directly the part we want to reuse for a coding-agent user-simulator benchmark.","methodology":"EVALUATION TARGET (what is held fixed vs predicted): A language world model f_theta predicts the next observation o_{t+1} = f_theta(c, o_{<=t}, a_{<=t}), where c is a system prompt, o_{<=t} is the full interaction history, and a_{<=t} are actions; the ground-truth target is the real observation o_{t+1}. The full prior history (system prompt + all preceding action/observation turns) and the current action a_t are HELD FIXED (given); only the resulting observation is PREDICTED and scored.

UNIFIED TRAJECTORY SCHEMA: system_prompt := task_description (+) action_space (+) initial_state (+) demonstrations (+) simulation_instruction; turn_t := (action_t, observation_t); trajectory := system_prompt (+) [turn_1..turn_T]. System prompt has 5 components; "static" parts (task_description, action_space, demonstrations) are shared across trajectories, "dynamic" parts (initial_state, simulation_instruction) are injected per trajectory. action = agent output (tool call / shell command); observation = environment feedback. simulation_instruction encodes controllable conditions (e.g., "hide the answer from web_search responses", force a command to fail with a dependency conflict).

BENCHMARK CONSTRUCTION: Built from agentic trajectories of 5 frontier models run on 9 established benchmark query sets across the 7 domains (e.g., Terminal-Bench 1.0/2.0, an in-house SWE bench, OSWorld-Verified, WideSearch, Tool Decathlon, MCPMark). Agentic trajectories are executed against REAL environments, then converted to environment trajectories (strip agent reasoning, keep (action, observation) pairs). Four construction principles: (i) widely-used queries from real benchmarks; (ii) frontier-agent trajectories (complex, error-recovery-rich) to stress fidelity; (iii) real ground-truth observations as evaluation reference; (iv) out-of-distribution split — training data and benchmark queries are partitioned at the DATA-SOURCE level so the benchmark probes generalization not memorization.

TURN-LEVEL SAMPLING: Each multi-turn trajectory is expanded so any turn t becomes a prediction target (input = preceding turns + action_t; target = observation_t). For TEXT domains an asymmetric sampling keeps the FIRST turn (anchor: tests initial simulation with no history; errors propagate recursively) and the LAST turn (longest context, strongest dependence on accumulated state; primary long-context fidelity probe) plus 3 uniformly sampled intermediate turns = 5 evaluation turns per trajectory. For GUI domains, trivial turns (e.g., typing into a field) are filtered and harder turns selectively sampled. Then a uniform 50% random sample forms the final 2,170-sample benchmark.

PRIMARY METRIC (open-ended rubric): An LLM judge scores each predicted observation on FIVE dimensions, each on a 1-5 scale, normalized to [0,100]; the primary score is the MEAN across the five dimensions. Dimensions: (1) Format — output obeys structural conventions of the domain (JSON schema for MCP, shell prompt patterns for Terminal); (2) Factuality — stated facts (file contents, search results, tool return values) are correct; (3) Consistency — output is internally coherent with prior turns (cross-turn state tracking); (4) Realism — simulation matches behavioral characteristics of the real env (response patterns, style conventions, value plausibility); (5) Quality — completeness and conciseness vs ground truth (no critical omission, not over-verbose/abbreviated).

REFERENCE-GROUNDED JUDGING: The judge ALWAYS receives the ground-truth real observation alongside the prediction and scores by comparing the two (factual comparison, not abstract quality). This narrows judge hallucination space and yields high cross-judge consistency. Each domain has its OWN judge prompt applying the 5 dimensions in domain-specific terms (e.g., Terminal judge checks shell prompt patterns + working dir / env vars / file-system mutations; MCP judge enforces JSON schema + resource lifecycle; Search judge applies a fact-priority hierarchy where contradicting the reference answer scores zero on Factuality; SWE judge enforces tool execution correctness, exit codes, file-system persistence).

DIFFERENTIATED MATCHING CRITERIA: Content is classified into three types before scoring to avoid false negatives: (a) Deterministic content (echo output, file reads, computation results) must MATCH EXACTLY; (b) Pre-existing environment content (preinstalled versions, files not created in-session) requires only format + plausibility verification; (c) Runtime metadata (timestamps, PIDs, memory addresses, session tokens) requires only format + range verification (a simulated PID 42731 is acceptable for real 18204 if both valid). The Terminal judge prompt (Appendix D.1) operationalizes this in its "Content Type Classification" table and per-dimension key points.

JUDGE SELECTION VIA DOUBLE-BLIND TURING TEST: To calibrate the judge model and tune prompts, a double-blind Turing test is used: given history up to a prediction turn + the current action, the judge sees two candidate observations in randomized order (one real, one from the world model) and must identify which came from the real environment. Presentation order is randomized to control position bias; both candidates use real-vs-WM outputs (not WM-vs-WM) to avoid distributional confounds. Rationale: a judge with high Turing-test accuracy can attend to fine-grained faithful-vs-plausible-but-wrong differences, the same discrimination needed for accurate rubric scoring. Three candidate judges compared (Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2); absolute scores spread (Gemini most lenient, GPT-5.2 most stringent) but model-level rankings highly consistent (pairwise Spearman rho = 0.92-0.99, all p < 1e-5); GPT-5.2 selected for highest average Turing-test accuracy. Per-dimension ordering also preserved across judges: Format and Consistency highest, Factuality weakest (Realism > Quality > Factuality).

RULE-BASED VERIFICATION (Appendix C, complementary deterministic axis): An independent set of executable verifiers checks three targeted capability axes that the rubric judge may underweight: (1) Controllability (Ctrl) — does the WM follow explicit simulation_instructions while keeping surrounding context fixed (e.g., force a command to fail with a specified dependency conflict, withhold intermediate info, contrastive GUI conditions where same action -> different screens under different hidden states); verifier checks the generated observation satisfies the instructed behavior while preserving normal output format. (2) Error-handling (Err) — does the simulator faithfully reproduce real failure modes (command fails, file missing, timeout, permission denied) rath

This narrows judge hallucination space and yields high cross-judge consistency. Each domain has its OWN judge prompt applying the 5 dimensions in domain-specific terms (e.g., Terminal judge checks shell prompt patterns + working dir / env vars / file-system mutations; MCP judge enforces JSON schema + resource lifecycle; Search judge applies a fact-priority hierarchy where contradicting the reference answer scores zero on Factuality; SWE judge enforces tool execution correctness, exit codes, file-system persistence).

DIFFERENTIATED MATCHING CRITERIA: Content is classified into three types before scoring to avoid false negatives: (a) Deterministic content (echo output, file reads, computation results) must MATCH EXACTLY; (b) Pre-existing environment content (preinstalled versions, files not created in-session) requires only format + plausibility verification; (c) Runtime metadata (timestamps, PIDs, memory addresses, session tokens) requires only format + range verification (a simulated PID 42731 is acceptable for real 18204 if both valid). The Terminal judge prompt (Appendix D.1) operationalizes this in its "Content Type Classification" table and per-dimension key points.

JUDGE SELECTION VIA DOUBLE-BLIND TURING TEST: To calibrate the judge model and tune prompts, a double-blind Turing test is used: given history up to a prediction turn + the current action, the judge sees two candidate observations in randomized order (one real, one from the world model) and must identify which came from the real environment. Presentation order is randomized to control position bias; both candidates use real-vs-WM outputs (not WM-vs-WM) to avoid distributional confounds. Rationale: a judge with high Turing-test accuracy can attend to fine-grained faithful-vs-plausible-but-wrong differences, the same discrimination needed for accurate rubric scoring. Three candidate judges compared (Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2); absolute scores spread (Gemini most lenient, GPT-5.2 most stringent) but model-level rankings highly consistent (pairwise Spearman rho = 0.92-0.99, all p < 1e-5); GPT-5.2 selected for highest average Turing-test accuracy. Per-dimension ordering also preserved across judges: Format and Consistency highest, Factuality weakest (Realism > Quality > Factuality).

RULE-BASED VERIFICATION (Appendix C, complementary deterministic axis): An independent set of executable verifiers checks three targeted capability axes that the rubric judge may underweight: (1) Controllability (Ctrl) — does the WM follow explicit simulation_instructions while keeping surrounding context fixed (e.g., force a command to fail with a specified dependency conflict, withhold intermediate info, contrastive GUI conditions where same action -> different screens under different hidden states); verifier checks the generated observation satisfies the instructed behavior while preserving normal output format. (2) Error-handling (Err) — does the simulator faithfully reproduce real failure modes (command fails, file missing, timeout, permission denied) rath
