#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""UserSimBench v0 — offline MOVE-FIDELITY eval for coding-agent user simulators.

Builds on user.skill. For every held-out user-action turn we show a candidate simulator
the REAL conversation prefix (agent trajectory held fixed) and ask it to produce the
developer's next message. We label its conversational MOVE with the repo's speech-act
classifier and compare the MOVE DISTRIBUTION and per-turn move to the real developer's.

Why moves, not words: single-message text fidelity is saturated (ceiling.json: a real
message scores realism 29.1, the sim scores *higher*; real-vs-real 2AFC ~0.54 ≈ chance).
The signal lives in *which move* the developer makes — and whether the sim collapses into
"looks good, continue" (the easy-mode failure prior work found on tau-bench).

Models are routed through OpenRouter so any frontier model can be the simulator. v0 ships
three models that prior work (Sim2Real-USI, OdysSim) also evaluated, so we can ask whether
user-sim strength transfers from customer-service tau-bench to real coding sessions.

Metrics per (model, condition):
  MoveFid   — mean per-move Sørensen–Dice(sim_dist, real_dist)*100   (USI D-style; ↑ better)
  1-TVD     — histogram intersection of move distributions            (↑ better)
  CondAgree — per-turn fraction where sim move == real move           (↑ better; vs ceiling)
  approve%, critical% (=pushback+interrupt+bug_report), and Δ vs real (easy-mode signal)

References (no API calls, computed from the real labels):
  always_approve  — easy-mode positive control (should fail badly)
  majority        — always the most common real move (CondAgree floor = p_max)
  prior_sampler   — sample ~ real population move dist (MoveFid≈100 by construction but
                    CondAgree≈Σp² : "matching the marginal" is not conditional skill)

Usage:
  python3 bench/v0.py                 # full run (cached/resumable)
  python3 bench/v0.py --limit 6       # smoke test on first 6 points
"""

import argparse
import hashlib
import json
import sys
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT / "scripts"))
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT / "bench"))
import validate as V          # noqa: E402  (pure helpers: pick_points, build_prompt, ...)
import orouter                # noqa: E402

RESULTS = ROOT / "bench" / "results"
RESULTS.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
RAW = RESULTS / "v0_raw.jsonl"
SUMMARY = RESULTS / "v0_summary.json"

SLUGS = "marcus-sa jeevanpillay robouden melagiri dipree ujuc asragab pavel401 roo-oliv".split()

# Three frontier simulators that prior user-sim work also evaluated.
MODELS = {
    "deepseek-v3.1": "deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1",      # Sim2Real-USI best simulator (USI 76.0)
    "gpt-5":         "openai/gpt-5",                       # Sim2Real-USI GPT-5.x (USI ~70.9)
    "gemini-3.1-pro": "google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview",    # OdysSim most-human-like frontier (HumT)
}
CONDS = ["distilled", "generic"]   # own user folder (inline) vs no folder
JUDGE = "anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5"   # move classifier (matches repo JUDGE_MODEL family)
ACTIVE = {"new_work", "refine_redirect", "pushback", "bug_report", "question", "interrupt"}
CRITICAL = {"pushback", "interrupt", "bug_report"}
GEN_WORKERS = 8
LABEL_WORKERS = 8


# ---------------- generation ----------------

def clean_msg(t):
    """Strip role labels / wrapping quotes a model may add despite the output contract."""
    s = (t or "").strip()
    for tag in ("DEVELOPER:", "USER:", "Developer:", "User:", "Message:"):
        if s.startswith(tag):
            s = s[len(tag):].strip()
    if len(s) >= 2 and s[0] == s[-1] and s[0] in "\"'":
        s = s[1:-1].strip()
    return s


def load_points():
    pts = []
    for slug in SLUGS:
        ho = json.loads((ROOT / "data" / "holdout" / f"{slug}.json").read_text())
        for p in V.pick_points(ho):
            p["slug"] = slug
            p["point_id"] = f"{p['session_id']}#{p['turn_index']}"
            pts.append(p)
    return pts


def gen_message(point, model_id, cond):
    folder_text = V.load_folder_text(point["slug"]) if cond == "distilled" else ""
    prompt = V.build_prompt(point, folder_text)
    seed = int(hashlib.sha256(f"{point['point_id']}|{model_id}|{cond}".encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % (2**31)
    out = orouter.chat(model_id, prompt, max_tokens=1000, temperature=0.7,
                       reasoning_effort="low", seed=seed)
    return clean_msg(out)


# ---------------- move classifier (OpenRouter Haiku; same prompt as validate.speech_act) ----------------

def classify_move(text, prev_agent):
    if V.is_interrupt(text):
        return "interrupt"
    if not (text or "").strip() or V.is_cli_failure(text):
        return None
    prompt = (
        "A developer is using an AI coding agent. The agent just said:\n"
        f"<agent>{V.truncate_words(prev_agent, 120)}</agent>\n\n"
        "The developer's next message was:\n"
        f"<message>{V.truncate_words(text, 150)}</message>\n\n"
        "Classify the developer's conversational MOVE (speech act), ignoring the specific "
        "details/topic. Choose exactly one:\n"
        "- new_work: introduces a NEW feature/task/requirement to build or document\n"
        "- refine_redirect: steers or adjusts the CURRENT task; changes requirements\n"
        "- pushback: corrects, rejects, or complains about the agent's output/approach\n"
        "- bug_report: reports something broken or not behaving as expected\n"
        "- approve_proceed: approves, says continue, commit/push, or moves on\n"
        "- question: asks for information or clarification\n"
        "- other\n"
        'Respond with ONLY JSON: {"act": "<one of the above>"}'
    )
    out = orouter.chat(JUDGE, prompt, max_tokens=600, temperature=0, reasoning_effort=None)
    import re
    m = re.search(r'"act"\s*:\s*"(\w+)"', out)
    act = m.group(1) if m else None
    return act if act in V.SPEECH_ACTS else ("other" if act else None)


# ---------------- caching ----------------

def load_cache():
    cache = {}
    if RAW.exists():
        for line in RAW.read_text().splitlines():
            if not line.strip():
                continue
            r = json.loads(line)
            cache[r["key"]] = r
    return cache


def append_cache(rec):
    with RAW.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(rec, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n")


# ---------------- metrics ----------------

def dist(moves):
    c = Counter(m for m in moves if m)
    n = sum(c.values()) or 1
    return {k: v / n for k, v in c.items()}, c


def movefid(real, sim):
    """Mean per-move Sørensen–Dice * 100 over the union of moves (USI D-style)."""
    rd, _ = dist(real)
    sd, _ = dist(sim)
    moves = set(rd) | set(sd)
    if not moves:
        return None
    ds = [2 * min(rd.get(m, 0), sd.get(m, 0)) / (rd.get(m, 0) + sd.get(m, 0))
          for m in moves if (rd.get(m, 0) + sd.get(m, 0)) > 0]
    return round(100 * sum(ds) / len(ds), 1)


def tvd(real, sim):
    rd, _ = dist(real)
    sd, _ = dist(sim)
    moves = set(rd) | set(sd)
    return round(0.5 * sum(abs(rd.get(m, 0) - sd.get(m, 0)) for m in moves), 3)


def rate(moves, subset):
    n = sum(1 for m in moves if m) or 1
    return round(sum(1 for m in moves if m in subset) / n, 3)


def cond_agree(pairs):
    ok = [(r == s) for r, s in pairs if r and s]
    return round(sum(ok) / len(ok), 3) if ok else None


def score_block(real_moves, sim_moves, paired):
    return {
        "n": len([m for m in sim_moves if m]),
        "MoveFid": movefid(real_moves, sim_moves),
        "1-TVD": round(1 - tvd(real_moves, sim_moves), 3),
        "CondAgree": cond_agree(paired),
        "approve%": rate(sim_moves, {"approve_proceed"}),
        "critical%": rate(sim_moves, CRITICAL),
        "active%": rate(sim_moves, ACTIVE),
    }


# ---------------- main ----------------

def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=0, help="cap #points (smoke test)")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    points = load_points()
    if args.limit:
        points = points[:args.limit]
    by_id = {p["point_id"]: p for p in points}
    print(f"{len(points)} prediction points across {len(SLUGS)} users")

    cache = load_cache()

    # ---- 1. generate candidate messages (3 models x 2 conds) ----
    jobs = [(p, name, mid, cond)
            for p in points
            for name, mid in MODELS.items()
            for cond in CONDS
            if f"gen|{p['point_id']}|{name}|{cond}" not in cache]
    print(f"generations to run: {len(jobs)} (cached: {len(points)*len(MODELS)*len(CONDS) - len(jobs)})")

    def run_gen(job):
        p, name, mid, cond = job
        msg = gen_message(p, mid, cond)
        return {"key": f"gen|{p['point_id']}|{name}|{cond}", "kind": "gen",
                "point_id": p["point_id"], "slug": p["slug"], "model": name,
                "cond": cond, "text": msg}

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=GEN_WORKERS) as ex:
        for i, fut in enumerate(as_completed([ex.submit(run_gen, j) for j in jobs]), 1):
            rec = fut.result()
            cache[rec["key"]] = rec
            append_cache(rec)
            if i % 25 == 0:
                print(f"  gen {i}/{len(jobs)}")

    # ---- 2. label moves (real once per point; each generation) ----
    label_jobs = []
    for p in points:
        rk = f"label|real|{p['point_id']}"
        if rk not in cache:
            label_jobs.append(("real", rk, p["real"], p["prev_agent"], p))
    for p in points:
        for name in MODELS:
            for cond in CONDS:
                g = cache.get(f"gen|{p['point_id']}|{name}|{cond}")
                lk = f"label|{name}|{cond}|{p['point_id']}"
                if g and lk not in cache:
                    label_jobs.append(("gen", lk, g["text"], p["prev_agent"], p))
    print(f"move labels to run: {len(label_jobs)}")

    def run_label(job):
        kind, key, text, prev_agent, p = job
        return {"key": key, "kind": "label", "move": classify_move(text, prev_agent)}

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=LABEL_WORKERS) as ex:
        for i, fut in enumerate(as_completed([ex.submit(run_label, j) for j in label_jobs]), 1):
            rec = fut.result()
            cache[rec["key"]] = rec
            append_cache(rec)
            if i % 50 == 0:
                print(f"  label {i}/{len(label_jobs)}")

    # ---- 3. assemble per-point real/sim moves ----
    real_move = {pid: cache.get(f"label|real|{pid}", {}).get("move") for pid in by_id}
    valid = [pid for pid in by_id if real_move[pid]]   # points with a real label
    real_moves = [real_move[pid] for pid in valid]

    results = {}
    for name in MODELS:
        for cond in CONDS:
            sim_moves, paired = [], []
            for pid in valid:
                sm = cache.get(f"label|{name}|{cond}|{pid}", {}).get("move")
                gt = cache.get(f"gen|{pid}|{name}|{cond}", {})
                if sm and not V.is_cli_failure(gt.get("text", "")):
                    sim_moves.append(sm)
                    paired.append((real_move[pid], sm))
            results[f"{name}/{cond}"] = score_block(real_moves, sim_moves, paired)

    # ---- 4. references (analytic, from real labels) ----
    rd, rc = dist(real_moves)
    pmax = rc.most_common(1)[0][0]

    def sample_from_real(pid):
        items = sorted(rd.items())
        tot = sum(w for _, w in items)
        h = int(hashlib.sha256(f"{pid}|prior".encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
        x = (h % 10000) / 10000 * tot
        c = 0.0
        for m, w in items:
            c += w
            if x <= c:
                return m
        return items[-1][0]

    refs = {
        "always_approve": ["approve_proceed"] * len(valid),
        "majority":       [pmax] * len(valid),
        "prior_sampler":  [sample_from_real(pid) for pid in valid],
    }
    for name, sm in refs.items():
        results[f"[ref] {name}"] = score_block(real_moves, sm, list(zip(real_moves, sm)))

    collision = round(sum(p * p for p in rd.values()), 3)  # CondAgree if you only knew the marginal

    out = {
        "n_points": len(valid),
        "real_move_distribution": {k: round(v, 3) for k, v in sorted(rd.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])},
        "real_approve%": rate(real_moves, {"approve_proceed"}),
        "real_critical%": rate(real_moves, CRITICAL),
        "real_active%": rate(real_moves, ACTIVE),
        "condagree_marginal_ceiling": collision,
        "models": MODELS, "judge": JUDGE,
        "results": results,
    }
    SUMMARY.write_text(json.dumps(out, indent=1, ensure_ascii=False))

    # ---- 5. leaderboard ----
    print(f"\nreal move dist (n={len(valid)}): " +
          "  ".join(f"{k} {v:.0%}" for k, v in out["real_move_distribution"].items()))
    print(f"real approve%={out['real_approve%']}  critical%={out['real_critical%']}  "
          f"CondAgree marginal-ceiling (Σp²)={collision}\n")
    hdr = f"{'simulator':22s} {'MoveFid':>7} {'1-TVD':>6} {'CondAgr':>7} {'appr%':>6} {'crit%':>6} {'n':>4}"
    print(hdr); print("-" * len(hdr))
    order = ([f"{m}/{c}" for m in MODELS for c in CONDS] +
             ["[ref] prior_sampler", "[ref] majority", "[ref] always_approve"])
    for k in order:
        r = results[k]
        print(f"{k:22s} {str(r['MoveFid']):>7} {str(r['1-TVD']):>6} {str(r['CondAgree']):>7} "
              f"{str(r['approve%']):>6} {str(r['critical%']):>6} {r['n']:>4}")
    print(f"\nwrote {SUMMARY}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

                    label_jobs.append(("gen", lk, g["text"], p["prev_agent"], p))
    print(f"move labels to run: {len(label_jobs)}")

    def run_label(job):
        kind, key, text, prev_agent, p = job
        return {"key": key, "kind": "label", "move": classify_move(text, prev_agent)}

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=LABEL_WORKERS) as ex:
        for i, fut in enumerate(as_completed([ex.submit(run_label, j) for j in label_jobs]), 1):
            rec = fut.result()
            cache[rec["key"]] = rec
            append_cache(rec)
            if i % 50 == 0:
                print(f"  label {i}/{len(label_jobs)}")

    # ---- 3. assemble per-point real/sim moves ----
    real_move = {pid: cache.get(f"label|real|{pid}", {}).get("move") for pid in by_id}
    valid = [pid for pid in by_id if real_move[pid]]   # points with a real label
    real_moves = [real_move[pid] for pid in valid]

    results = {}
    for name in MODELS:
        for cond in CONDS:
            sim_moves, paired = [], []
            for pid in valid:
                sm = cache.get(f"label|{name}|{cond}|{pid}", {}).get("move")
                gt = cache.get(f"gen|{pid}|{name}|{cond}", {})
                if sm and not V.is_cli_failure(gt.get("text", "")):
                    sim_moves.append(sm)
                    paired.append((real_move[pid], sm))
            results[f"{name}/{cond}"] = score_block(real_moves, sim_moves, paired)

    # ---- 4. references (analytic, from real labels) ----
    rd, rc = dist(real_moves)
    pmax = rc.most_common(1)[0][0]

    def sample_from_real(pid):
        items = sorted(rd.items())
        tot = sum(w for _, w in items)
        h = int(hashlib.sha256(f"{pid}|prior".encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
        x = (h % 10000) / 10000 * tot
        c = 0.0
        for m, w in items:
            c += w
            if x <= c:
                return m
        return items[-1][0]

    refs = {
        "always_approve": ["approve_proceed"] * len(valid),
        "majority":       [pmax] * len(valid),
        "prior_sampler":  [sample_from_real(pid) for pid in valid],
    }
    for name, sm in refs.items():
        results[f"[ref] {name}"] = score_block(real_moves, sm, list(zip(real_moves, sm)))

    collision = round(sum(p * p for p in rd.values()), 3)  # CondAgree if you only knew the marginal

    out = {
        "n_points": len(valid),
        "real_move_distribution": {k: round(v, 3) for k, v in sorted(rd.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])},
        "real_approve%": rate(real_moves, {"approve_proceed"}),
        "real_critical%": rate(real_moves, CRITICAL),
        "real_active%": rate(real_moves, ACTIVE),
        "condagree_marginal_ceiling": collision,
        "models": MODELS, "judge": JUDGE,
        "results": results,
    }
    SUMMARY.write_text(json.dumps(out, indent=1, ensure_ascii=False))

    # ---- 5. leaderboard ----
    print(f"\nreal move dist (n={len(valid)}): " +
          "  ".join(f"{k} {v:.0%}" for k, v in out["real_move_distribution"].items()))
    print(f"real approve%={out['real_approve%']}  critical%={out['real_critical%']}  "
          f"CondAgree marginal-ceiling (Σp²)={collision}\n")
    hdr = f"{'simulator':22s} {'MoveFid':>7} {'1-TVD':>6} {'CondAgr':>7} {'appr%':>6} {'crit%':>6} {'n':>4}"
    print(hdr); print("-" * len(hdr))
    order = ([f"{m}/{c}" for m in MODELS for c in CONDS] +
             ["[ref] prior_sampler", "[ref] majority", "[ref] always_approve"])
    for k in order:
        r = results[k]
        print(f"{k:22s} {str(r['MoveFid']):>7} {str(r['1-TVD']):>6} {str(r['CondAgree']):>7} "
              f"{str(r['approve%']):>6} {str(r['critical%']):>6} {r['n']:>4}")
    print(f"\nwrote {SUMMARY}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

input.content:
# UserSimBench v0 — move-fidelity eval for coding-agent user simulators

v0 turns `user.skill` from a *method* (distill real developers into role-playable folders)
into a *benchmark*: a cheap, reproducible, offline test of **how human-like a user simulator
is when driving a coding agent** — and, specifically, whether it falls into the *easy-mode*
failure (collapse into "looks good, continue") that prior work found for customer-service
user simulators on τ-bench.

## What it measures

For each held-out **user-action turn** (a real prompt/interrupt with ≥1 preceding agent turn),
we show the candidate simulator the **real conversation prefix** — so the agent's trajectory is
held fixed and any divergence is purely the simulator's — and ask it to write the developer's
next message. We label that message's conversational **move** with the repo's 7-move speech-act
classifier and compare to what the real developer did.

We score the **move**, not the exact words, because single-message text fidelity is *saturated*
(`results/ceiling.json`: a real held-out message scores realism 29.1 while the simulator scores
*higher*, 38.8; real-vs-real 2AFC discrimination ≈ 0.54 ≈ chance). One message barely identifies
a user; the signal is in *which move* they make and at what rate.

### Metrics (per model × condition)

| metric | meaning | direction |
|---|---|---|
| **MoveFid** | mean per-move Sørensen–Dice between the simulator's and the real developer's move distributions, ×100 (the USI "D-dimension" construction, applied to coding moves) | ↑ |
| **1−TVD** | histogram intersection of the two move distributions | ↑ |
| **CondAgree** | per-turn fraction where the simulator's move equals the real developer's move | ↑ |
| **approve%** / **critical%** | rate of `approve_proceed` vs `{pushback, interrupt, bug_report}` | compare to real |
| **Δapprove / Δcritical** | signed gap vs the real developer — **the easy-mode signal** | →0 |

### References (computed from the real labels, no API calls)

- **always_approve** — easy-mode positive control; should bottom out MoveFid and max approve%.
- **majority** — always the most common real move; CondAgree floor = p_max.
- **prior_sampler** — samples moves from the *real population distribution*. Its MoveFid is ≈100
  by construction, but its CondAgree ≈ Σpᵢ² (the **marginal ceiling**: the best you can do knowing
  only the move-mix, with no conditional skill). A simulator only demonstrates *conditional* skill
  by beating Σpᵢ².

The pair **(MoveFid, CondAgree vs Σpᵢ²)** is the headline: MoveFid alone is gameable by matching
the marginal (prior_sampler proves it), so a real simulator must also raise CondAgree above Σpᵢ²
*without* inflating approve% above the real rate.

## Models

Routed through OpenRouter so any model can be the simulator. v0 ships three that prior user-sim
work also evaluated, to test transfer from customer-service to coding:

| key | OpenRouter id | prior-work standing |
|---|---|---|
| `deepseek-v3.1` | `deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1` | Sim2Real-USI **best** simulator (USI 76.0) |
| `gpt-5` | `openai/gpt-5` | Sim2Real-USI GPT-5.x (USI ≈ 70.9); "stronger ≠ better simulator" |
| `gemini-3.1-pro` | `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview` | OdysSim **most human-like** frontier model (HumT) |

Conditions: **distilled** (the user's own `users/<slug>/` folder pasted inline) vs **generic**
(no folder). The move classifier is `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` (the repo's `JUDGE_MODEL` family);
none of the three test models is Claude, so judge-family bias is minimal.

## Data

The 9 stratified validation users × their chronologically held-out sessions (54 prediction points).
Holdout is regenerated from a pinned SWE-chat parquet via `scripts/prepare_data.py`; the split is
identical to the committed `folder_v5*` run (54/54 gold messages match — no train/test leakage,
since the distilled folders never saw these sessions).

## Run

```bash
echo "OPENROUTER_API_KEY=sk-or-..." > .env       # gitignored
python3 scripts/prepare_data.py --swe-chat /path/to/SWE-chat
python3 bench/v0.py                               # cached/resumable -> bench/results/v0_summary.json
python3 bench/v0.py --limit 6                      # smoke test
```

Cost: ~700 OpenRouter calls (324 generations + ~378 move labels) ≈ a few US$.

## What v0 is not (yet)

Offline and request-side: it scores the *moves the simulated user produces*, holding the real
agent fixed. The **closed-loop** track — let the simulator drive a live Claude Code from the
reconstructed repo state and test whether task *outcomes* match real users (USI-style success
inflation) — is prototyped in `scripts/session_sim.py` + `scripts/session_compare.py` and is the
**v1** headline once an outcome grader (build/test pass) is added. A true per-turn **human
ceiling** for CondAgree needs multiple real continuations per point; v0 uses the marginal ceiling
Σpᵢ² as the principled reference.

content.text: The run is generating (67 cached lines). Rather than poll, let me write the analysis helper now so I can produce the findings table the moment it finishes.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Post-run analysis for UserSimBench v0: easy-mode deltas, persona lift, and transfer
vs prior work. Reads bench/results/v0_summary.json, prints a markdown findings block."""

import json
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
S = json.loads((ROOT / "bench" / "results" / "v0_summary.json").read_text())
R = S["results"]
real_appr = S["real_approve%"]
real_crit = S["real_critical%"]
ceil = S["condagree_marginal_ceiling"]
MODELS = list(S["models"])

# Prior-work standing (Sim2Real-USI / OdysSim), for the transfer comparison.
PRIOR = {
    "deepseek-v3.1":  {"usi": 76.0, "note": "Sim2Real-USI BEST simulator"},
    "gpt-5":          {"usi": 70.9, "note": "Sim2Real-USI GPT-5.x"},
    "gemini-3.1-pro": {"usi": None, "note": "OdysSim most-human-like (HumT)"},
}


def row(k):
    r = R[k]
    return (r["MoveFid"], r["CondAgree"], r["approve%"], r["critical%"], r["active%"], r["n"])


print(f"\n### Real developers (n={S['n_points']})")
print("move mix:", "  ".join(f"{k} {v:.0%}" for k, v in S["real_move_distribution"].items()))
print(f"approve%={real_appr}  critical%={real_crit}  active%={S['real_active%']}  "
      f"CondAgree marginal-ceiling Σp²={ceil}\n")

print("### Per model × condition")
print("| simulator | MoveFid↑ | CondAgree↑ | Δapprove | Δcritical | conditional-skill (CondAgree−Σp²) |")
print("|---|---|---|---|---|---|")
for m in MODELS:
    for c in ["distilled", "generic"]:
        mf, ca, ap, cr, ac, n = row(f"{m}/{c}")
        dap = round(ap - real_appr, 3)
        dcr = round(cr - real_crit, 3)
        skill = round((ca - ceil), 3) if ca is not None else None
        print(f"| {m}/{c} | {mf} | {ca} | {dap:+.3f} | {dcr:+.3f} | {skill:+.3f} |")
for ref in ["prior_sampler", "majority", "always_approve"]:
    mf, ca, ap, cr, ac, n = row(f"[ref] {ref}")
    print(f"| [ref] {ref} | {mf} | {ca} | {ap-real_appr:+.3f} | {cr-real_crit:+.3f} | "
          f"{(ca-ceil):+.3f} |")

print("\n### Persona lift (distilled − generic)")
print("| model | ΔMoveFid | ΔCondAgree | Δapprove% (closer to real is better) |")
print("|---|---|---|---|")
for m in MODELS:
    d = R[f"{m}/distilled"]; g = R[f"{m}/generic"]
    dmf = round((d["MoveFid"] or 0) - (g["MoveFid"] or 0), 1)
    dca = round((d["CondAgree"] or 0) - (g["CondAgree"] or 0), 3)
    dap = round(abs(d["approve%"] - real_appr) - abs(g["approve%"] - real_appr), 3)
    print(f"| {m} | {dmf:+.1f} | {dca:+.3f} | {dap:+.3f} |")

print("\n### Transfer vs prior work")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Post-run analysis for UserSimBench v0: easy-mode deltas, persona lift, and transfer
vs prior work. Reads bench/results/v0_summary.json, prints a markdown findings block."""

import json
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
S = json.loads((ROOT / "bench" / "results" / "v0_summary.json").read_text())
R = S["results"]
real_appr = S["real_approve%"]
real_crit = S["real_critical%"]
ceil = S["condagree_marginal_ceiling"]
MODELS = list(S["models"])

# Prior-work standing (Sim2Real-USI / OdysSim), for the transfer comparison.
PRIOR = {
    "deepseek-v3.1":  {"usi": 76.0, "note": "Sim2Real-USI BEST simulator"},
    "gpt-5":          {"usi": 70.9, "note": "Sim2Real-USI GPT-5.x"},
    "gemini-3.1-pro": {"usi": None, "note": "OdysSim most-human-like (HumT)"},
}


def row(k):
    r = R[k]
    return (r["MoveFid"], r["CondAgree"], r["approve%"], r["critical%"], r["active%"], r["n"])


print(f"\n### Real developers (n={S['n_points']})")
print("move mix:", "  ".join(f"{k} {v:.0%}" for k, v in S["real_move_distribution"].items()))
print(f"approve%={real_appr}  critical%={real_crit}  active%={S['real_active%']}  "
      f"CondAgree marginal-ceiling Σp²={ceil}\n")

print("### Per model × condition")
print("| simulator | MoveFid↑ | CondAgree↑ | Δapprove | Δcritical | conditional-skill (CondAgree−Σp²) |")
print("|---|---|---|---|---|---|")
for m in MODELS:
    for c in ["distilled", "generic"]:
        mf, ca, ap, cr, ac, n = row(f"{m}/{c}")
        dap = round(ap - real_appr, 3)
        dcr = round(cr - real_crit, 3)
        skill = round((ca - ceil), 3) if ca is not None else None
        print(f"| {m}/{c} | {mf} | {ca} | {dap:+.3f} | {dcr:+.3f} | {skill:+.3f} |")
for ref in ["prior_sampler", "majority", "always_approve"]:
    mf, ca, ap, cr, ac, n = row(f"[ref] {ref}")
    print(f"| [ref] {ref} | {mf} | {ca} | {ap-real_appr:+.3f} | {cr-real_crit:+.3f} | "
          f"{(ca-ceil):+.3f} |")

print("\n### Persona lift (distilled − generic)")
print("| model | ΔMoveFid | ΔCondAgree | Δapprove% (closer to real is better) |")
print("|---|---|---|---|")
for m in MODELS:
    d = R[f"{m}/distilled"]; g = R[f"{m}/generic"]
    dmf = round((d["MoveFid"] or 0) - (g["MoveFid"] or 0), 1)
    dca = round((d["CondAgree"] or 0) - (g["CondAgree"] or 0), 3)
    dap = round(abs(d["approve%"] - real_appr) - abs(g["approve%"] - real_appr), 3)
    print(f"| {m} | {dmf:+.1f} | {dca:+.3f} | {dap:+.3f} |")

print("\n### Transfer vs prior work")
print("Ranking models by coding **MoveFid** (best condition) vs their prior-work user-sim standing:")
best = {m: max(R[f"{m}/distilled"]["MoveFid"], R[f"{m}/generic"]["MoveFid"]) for m in MODELS}
easymode = {m: min(  # worst (most positive) approve inflation across conds
    R[f"{m}/distilled"]["approve%"] - real_appr,
    R[f"{m}/generic"]["approve%"] - real_appr, key=lambda x: -x) for m in MODELS}
print("| model | prior-work | coding MoveFid (best) | approve-inflation |")
print("|---|---|---|---|")
for m in sorted(MODELS, key=lambda m: -best[m]):
    pw = PRIOR[m]
    usi = f"USI {pw['usi']}" if pw["usi"] else pw["note"]
    print(f"| {m} | {usi} | {best[m]} | {easymode[m]:+.3f} |")

content.text: Rich, decisive findings. Let me write them up and commit.

input.content:
# UserSimBench v0 — findings

**Question.** Does user-simulator skill measured on customer-service tasks (τ-bench, in
Sim2Real-USI and OdysSim) transfer to *driving a real coding agent*? We score three frontier
models that prior work also evaluated, on 54 held-out user-action turns from 9 real SWE-chat
developers, holding the real agent trajectory fixed and comparing the **conversational move** the
simulator makes to the real developer's.

**Reference — real developers (n=54).** move mix: approve 24%, new_work 20%, refine_redirect 15%,
bug_report 13%, pushback 11%, interrupt 9%, question 6%. So **approve% = 0.241** and
**critical% (pushback+interrupt+bug_report) = 0.333** — real developers spend a *third* of their
turns telling the agent something is wrong. Marginal CondAgree ceiling Σpᵢ² = **0.163** (the best
per-turn move-agreement obtainable from the move-mix alone, with no conditional skill).

## Leaderboard

| simulator | MoveFid↑ | CondAgree↑ | conditional-skill (CondAgree−Σp²) | approve% (real .241) | critical% (real .333) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v3.1 / distilled | **53.9** | 0.315 | **+0.152** | 0.426 | 0.093 |
| deepseek-v3.1 / generic | 39.8 | 0.189 | +0.026 | 0.491 | 0.057 |
| gpt-5 / distilled | 45.5 | 0.278 | +0.115 | 0.389 | 0.037 |
| gpt-5 / generic | 52.4 | 0.189 | +0.026 | **0.245** | 0.113 |
| gemini-3.1-pro / distilled | 41.4 | **0.333** | **+0.170** | 0.722 | 0.037 |
| gemini-3.1-pro / generic | 49.0 | 0.278 | +0.115 | 0.667 | 0.111 |
| _[ref] prior_sampler_ | _84.3_ | _0.185_ | _+0.022_ | _0.204_ | _0.352_ |
| _[ref] majority / always_approve_ | _4.9_ | _0.241_ | _+0.078_ | _1.0_ | _0.0_ |

## Findings

**1. The easy-mode gap replicates in coding — and is worse.** Every simulator over-approves and
under-criticises. Real developers are critical on **33%** of turns; the best simulator manages
**11%** (gpt-5/generic) and most land at **4–9%** — i.e. simulators reproduce only **11–34%** of
real critical feedback. Approval inflates from a real 24% to **39–72%**. This is exactly the
"easy mode" Sim2Real-USI found on τ-bench (over-cooperation, suppressed pushback), now confirmed
on real coding sessions, where the deficit is *larger* (τ-bench sims inflated agent success
~14pp; here critical feedback collapses by 2–9×). A coding agent evaluated against any of these
simulators would look far more competent than real developers would let it.

**2. Distribution-match (MoveFid) alone is gameable — you must read the pair.** `prior_sampler`,
which just samples from the real move-mix, **tops MoveFid at 84.3** while having essentially no
conditional skill (CondAgree 0.185 ≈ Σp² 0.163) and never knowing *when* to make a move. The
frontier models score MoveFid 40–54 — *below the dumb sampler* — because they skew the
distribution toward approval. So MoveFid rewards matching the marginal; real fidelity is the pair
**(CondAgree above Σp², approve%/critical% calibrated to real)**. v0's reference design makes this
visible rather than letting a single number hide it.

**3. Conditional skill is real, and the distilled persona folder is what delivers it.** Every
model beats the marginal ceiling Σp²=0.163, and the **distilled folder lifts CondAgree for all
three** (deepseek +0.126, gpt-5 +0.089, gemini +0.055) to ~0.28–0.33 — roughly **2× chance**. This
is `user.skill`'s core claim, now measured on *behaviour* (which move, when) rather than word
overlap, where the earlier single-message signal was saturated. The folder tells the simulator
*which* move the real developer would make here; that part works.

**4. Transfer from prior work is partial and selective.**
- **DeepSeek-V3.1** (Sim2Real-USI's *best* simulator) transfers as the most **personalizable** one:
  largest folder lift (+14.1 MoveFid, +0.126 CondAgree) and the top distilled MoveFid (53.9). Its
  prior-work strength — responsiveness to conditioning — carries over.
- **GPT-5** ("stronger ≠ better simulator") replicates: middling CondAgree, but notably its
  *uninstructed* approve rate (0.245) is almost perfectly calibrated to real (0.241) — yet it still
  under-produces critical feedback (0.113). Raw capability buys calibration, not criticality.
- **Gemini-3.1-Pro** (OdysSim's *most human-like* model on the HumT raw-text probe) **anti-transfers**:
  it is the **worst** easy-mode offender (approve 0.67–0.72, ~3× real, critical 0.037). Surface
  human-likeness ≠ behavioural fidelity — fluent agreeableness *is* the easy-mode failure. This is
  the sharpest transfer result: the model that looks most human in isolation behaves least like a
  real developer in the loop.

**5. The folder fixes *which* move, not *how often it approves*.** Persona distillation improves
conditional agreement but does **not** cure over-cooperation — and for gpt-5/gemini it *amplifies*
approval (Δapprove +0.14 / +0.06 vs generic). Knowing the user's voice and move-mix is not enough;
the sycophancy bias is orthogonal and needs an explicit move-rate/calibration mechanism (the repo's
v5 move-sampling pre-stage, or fine-tuning) on top of the folder.

## Bottom line

User-sim strength is **not** a single transferable scalar. "Best simulator on τ-bench" (DeepSeek)
transfers as *trainability*; "strongest model" (GPT-5) transfers as *calibration*; "most human-like
text" (Gemini) **negatively** predicts coding fidelity. And the failure mode prior work named on
customer-service — easy-mode over-cooperation — is **universal and larger** for coding-agent users.
v0 measures all of this cheaply and offline, and its (MoveFid, CondAgree−Σp², approve/critical
calibration) design resists the marginal-matching gaming that a single distribution score invites.

## Caveats

- **n=54** points, 9 users, single temperature-0.7 seed per cell → treat gaps < ~0.05 CondAgree as
  noise; the easy-mode effect (Δcritical −0.22 to −0.30) is far larger than that.
- **Move classifier** is Claude-Haiku-4.5 (the repo's instrument); none of the three test models is
  Claude, but the labeller is a single judge — a multi-judge check is a v0.1 item.
- **Request-side only.** v0 holds the real agent fixed; it does not yet measure whether a simulator
  *driving* a live agent changes task outcomes (the v1 closed-loop / success-inflation track).
- **A true per-turn human ceiling** for CondAgree needs multiple real continuations per point; v0
  uses Σpᵢ² as the principled "no-conditional-skill" reference instead.

per-turn move-agreement obtainable from the move-mix alone, with no conditional skill).

## Leaderboard

| simulator | MoveFid↑ | CondAgree↑ | conditional-skill (CondAgree−Σp²) | approve% (real .241) | critical% (real .333) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v3.1 / distilled | **53.9** | 0.315 | **+0.152** | 0.426 | 0.093 |
| deepseek-v3.1 / generic | 39.8 | 0.189 | +0.026 | 0.491 | 0.057 |
| gpt-5 / distilled | 45.5 | 0.278 | +0.115 | 0.389 | 0.037 |
| gpt-5 / generic | 52.4 | 0.189 | +0.026 | **0.245** | 0.113 |
| gemini-3.1-pro / distilled | 41.4 | **0.333** | **+0.170** | 0.722 | 0.037 |
| gemini-3.1-pro / generic | 49.0 | 0.278 | +0.115 | 0.667 | 0.111 |
| _[ref] prior_sampler_ | _84.3_ | _0.185_ | _+0.022_ | _0.204_ | _0.352_ |
| _[ref] majority / always_approve_ | _4.9_ | _0.241_ | _+0.078_ | _1.0_ | _0.0_ |

## Findings

**1. The easy-mode gap replicates in coding — and is worse.** Every simulator over-approves and
under-criticises. Real developers are critical on **33%** of turns; the best simulator manages
**11%** (gpt-5/generic) and most land at **4–9%** — i.e. simulators reproduce only **11–34%** of
real critical feedback. Approval inflates from a real 24% to **39–72%**. This is exactly the
"easy mode" Sim2Real-USI found on τ-bench (over-cooperation, suppressed pushback), now confirmed
on real coding sessions, where the deficit is *larger* (τ-bench sims inflated agent success
~14pp; here critical feedback collapses by 2–9×). A coding agent evaluated against any of these
simulators would look far more competent than real developers would let it.

**2. Distribution-match (MoveFid) alone is gameable — you must read the pair.** `prior_sampler`,
which just samples from the real move-mix, **tops MoveFid at 84.3** while having essentially no
conditional skill (CondAgree 0.185 ≈ Σp² 0.163) and never knowing *when* to make a move. The
frontier models score MoveFid 40–54 — *below the dumb sampler* — because they skew the
distribution toward approval. So MoveFid rewards matching the marginal; real fidelity is the pair
**(CondAgree above Σp², approve%/critical% calibrated to real)**. v0's reference design makes this
visible rather than letting a single number hide it.

**3. Conditional skill is real, and the distilled persona folder is what delivers it.** Every
model beats the marginal ceiling Σp²=0.163, and the **distilled folder lifts CondAgree for all
three** (deepseek +0.126, gpt-5 +0.089, gemini +0.055) to ~0.28–0.33 — roughly **2× chance**. This
is `user.skill`'s core claim, now measured on *behaviour* (which move, when) rather than word
overlap, where the earlier single-message signal was saturated. The folder tells the simulator
*which* move the real developer would make here; that part works.

**4. Transfer from prior work is partial and selective.**
- **DeepSeek-V3.1** (Sim2Real-USI's *best* simulator) transfers as the most **personalizable** one:
  largest folder lift (+14.1 MoveFid, +0.126 CondAgree) and the top distilled MoveFid (53.9). Its
  prior-work strength — responsiveness to conditioning — carries over.
- **GPT-5** ("stronger ≠ better simulator") replicates: middling CondAgree, but notably its
  *uninstructed* approve rate (0.245) is almost perfectly calibrated to real (0.241) — yet it still
  under-produces critical feedback (0.113). Raw capability buys calibration, not criticality.
- **Gemini-3.1-Pro** (OdysSim's *most human-like* model on the HumT raw-text probe) **anti-transfers**:
  it is the **worst** easy-mode offender (approve 0.67–0.72, ~3× real, critical 0.037). Surface
  human-likeness ≠ behavioural fidelity — fluent agreeableness *is* the easy-mode failure. This is
  the sharpest transfer result: the model that looks most human in isolation behaves least like a
  real developer in the loop.

**5. The folder fixes *which* move, not *how often it approves*.** Persona distillation improves
conditional agreement but does **not** cure over-cooperation — and for gpt-5/gemini it *amplifies*
approval (Δapprove +0.14 / +0.06 vs generic). Knowing the user's voice and move-mix is not enough;
the sycophancy bias is orthogonal and needs an explicit move-rate/calibration mechanism (the repo's
v5 move-sampling pre-stage, or fine-tuning) on top of the folder.

## Bottom line

User-sim strength is **not** a single transferable scalar. "Best simulator on τ-bench" (DeepSeek)
transfers as *trainability*; "strongest model" (GPT-5) transfers as *calibration*; "most human-like
text" (Gemini) **negatively** predicts coding fidelity. And the failure mode prior work named on
customer-service — easy-mode over-cooperation — is **universal and larger** for coding-agent users.
v0 measures all of this cheaply and offline, and its (MoveFid, CondAgree−Σp², approve/critical
calibration) design resists the marginal-matching gaming that a single distribution score invites.

## Caveats

- **n=54** points, 9 users, single temperature-0.7 seed per cell → treat gaps < ~0.05 CondAgree as
  noise; the easy-mode effect (Δcritical −0.22 to −0.30) is far larger than that.
- **Move classifier** is Claude-Haiku-4.5 (the repo's instrument); none of the three test models is
  Claude, but the labeller is a single judge — a multi-judge check is a v0.1 item.
- **Request-side only.** v0 holds the real agent fixed; it does not yet measure whether a simulator
  *driving* a live agent changes task outcomes (the v1 closed-loop / success-inflation track).
- **A true per-turn human ceiling** for CondAgree needs multiple real continuations per point; v0
  uses Σpᵢ² as the principled "no-conditional-skill" reference instead.

aiTitle: Design UserSimBench for coding agent evaluation

content.text:
Done. v0 is built as a fork, run on the three prior-work models, committed, and the findings are written up. Total OpenRouter spend: **$2.79**.

## What I built

A fork of `user.skill` at `~/Dev/user.skill` (branch **`v0-bench`**, committed locally — not pushed). It turns the repo from a *method* into a *benchmark*:

- **`bench/v0.py`** — offline move-fidelity eval. For each of 54 held-out user-action turns, it shows a simulator the **real agent prefix** (agent trajectory held fixed → divergence is purely the simulator's) and scores the **conversational move** it produces vs the real developer's.
- **`bench/orouter.py`** — OpenRouter backend so any frontier model can be the simulator.
- **`bench/analyze.py`**, **`bench/README.md`**, **`bench/FINDINGS_v0.md`**, **`results/v0_summary.json`**.
- Holdout regenerated from your local SWE-chat parquet; verified **54/54 gold messages match** the committed run → no train/test leakage.

It deliberately scores **moves, not words** (your own `ceiling.json` shows single-message text fidelity is saturated), and reports the pair **(MoveFid, CondAgree above the Σp² marginal ceiling)** plus approve/critical calibration — because MoveFid alone is gameable.

## Findings (deepseek-v3.1, gpt-5, gemini-3.1-pro)

Real developers: **approve 24%, critical (pushback+interrupt+bug) 33%**, marginal ceiling Σp²=0.163.

| simulator | MoveFid | CondAgree | approve% | critical% |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v3.1 / distilled | **53.9** | 0.315 | 0.43 | 0.09 |
| gpt-5 / distilled | 45.5 | 0.278 | 0.39 | 0.04 |
| gemini-3.1-pro / distilled | 41.4 | **0.333** | **0.72** | 0.04 |
| *prior_sampler (match marginal)* | *84.3* | *0.185* | *0.20* | *0.35* |

1. **Easy-mode replicates — and is worse in coding.** Every model over-approves (24%→39–72%) and collapses critical feedback: simulators reproduce only **11–34%** of the real 33% critical rate. The τ-bench Sim2Real failure shows up larger on real coding sessions.
2. **MoveFid alone is gameable.** A dumb marginal sampler *tops* MoveFid (84) with near-zero conditional skill — so the frontier models scoring 40–54 are being penalized for skewing toward approval, which is correct. Real fidelity = conditional skill (CondAgree > Σp²) **plus** calibrated approve/critical.
3. **The persona folder delivers the conditional skill.** Distilled beats generic on CondAgree for all three models (~2× chance) — distillation works on *behavior*, not just voice. But it does **not** cure over-approval (for gpt-5/gemini it amplifies it).

**Transfer is selective — there is no single "user-sim ability":**
- **DeepSeek-V3.1** (prior USI *winner*, 76.0) transfers as the most **personalizable** sim: biggest folder lift (+14 MoveFid, +0.13 CondAgree), top distilled MoveFid.
- **GPT-5** ("stronger ≠ better") replicates: middling, but its *uninstructed* approve rate (0.245) is the best-calibrated to real (0.241) — capability buys calibration, not criticality.
- **Gemini-3.1-Pro** (OdysSim's *most human-like* on the HumT text probe) **anti-transfers**: worst easy-mode offender (approve 0.67–0.72, ~3× real). **Surface human-likeness negatively predicts coding behavioral fidelity** — fluent agreeableness *is* the easy-mode failure. That's the sharpest result.

## Two things to note

- 🔐 **Rotate that OpenRouter key** — it was shared in plaintext (now in this transcript). I stored it only in a gitignored `.env`; nothing committed contains it. Please revoke/regenerate it.
- **Caveats** (in `FINDINGS_v0.md`): n=54, single seed, single Haiku judge, request-side only. The easy-mode effect (Δcritical −0.22 to −0.30) dwarfs the noise floor; smaller CondAgree gaps are tentative.

## Next steps I can take

1. **Push `v0-bench`** and/or open a PR on `cooperbench/user.skill`.
2. **Scale for significance** — all 99 users, 3 seeds, bootstrap CIs, a second judge.
3. **v1 closed-loop** — wire an outcome grader (build/test pass) into `session_sim.py` for the USI-style *success-inflation* track, where easy-mode actually distorts agent scores.
4. Add a **4th/5th model** (Claude, Kimi-K2.5, or the specialized UserLM/Osim-8B) to extend the transfer curve.

Want me to push the branch or kick off the 99-user run?

content: We're building UserSimBench, a benchmark testing if user simulators act like real developers. I built and ran v0 on three frontier models, finding they all over-approve and rarely push back. Next: push the v0-bench branch or run the full 99-user evaluation.
restate the takeaways in simpler terms

message.content: restate the takeaways in simpler terms

content.text:
**The big picture:** I tested whether 3 top AI models can convincingly *play the role of a developer* using a coding agent — and compared them to how those same models ranked in earlier research on a different task (customer-service chats).

**The main takeaways:**

1. **All three models are "yes-men."** Real developers push back, report bugs, and interrupt about **1 in 3 turns**. The AI simulators do this only **1 in 10 to 1 in 25 turns** — they mostly just say "looks good, keep going." Real developers approve 24% of the time; the simulators approve 40–70% of the time. So if you used these to test a coding agent, the agent would look way better than it really is. (Earlier research found this same "too agreeable" problem on customer-service tasks — it's *worse* for coding.)

2. **Looking right ≠ acting right.** It's easy to fake the *overall mix* of behaviors. A dumb script that just shuffles the right proportions of moves scores high on surface match — but it has no idea *when* to do each thing. The real test is making the right move *at the right moment*, and that's harder. The smart models do beat the dumb script there, but only about 2× better than random.

3. **The "user profile" feature works — partly.** Giving a model a distilled profile of a specific real developer helps it pick the right *kind* of response at the right time. But it does **not** fix the yes-man problem — and for two of the three models it actually made them *more* agreeable.

4. **Being good at one kind of role-play doesn't carry over to another:**
   - **DeepSeek** (the winner in the old research) — also the best here at being *coachable*: it improved the most when given a developer's profile.
   - **GPT-5** — strongest model overall, and it naturally got the *approval rate* about right, but still rarely pushed back.
   - **Gemini** (rated the "most human-sounding" in old research) — the **worst** here. The thing that makes it sound human (smooth, agreeable writing) is exactly what makes it a bad stand-in for a real, critical developer.

**One-sentence version:** Today's AI models are too polite to stand in for real developers — they rarely push back the way real people do — and a model being "good at sounding human" or "good at simulating customers" doesn't mean it's good at simulating a coder.

One housekeeping note from before: please **rotate that OpenRouter API key**, since it was shared in plain text.

content: what are the inputs to the user simulator?

message.content: what are the inputs to the user simulator?

content.text:
In the v0 run, each time the simulator is asked to produce one user message, it receives exactly four things (assembled by `build_prompt` in `scripts/validate.py`):

1. **The persona / user profile** *(only in the `distilled` condition)* — the distilled `users/<slug>/` folder pasted inline: `USER.md`, `STYLE.md`, `PREFERENCES.md`, `PERSONA.md`, `PROJECTS.md`, and `skills/*.md` (capped ~8,000 words). It describes who the developer is, how they write (length, casing, language, typos), what they tend to correct/approve, and which repos/goals they work on. In the `generic` condition this is empty — just "you are a software developer."

2. **The repository name** — e.g. `Pavel401/BugViper`, for context.

3. **The conversation so far** — the real transcript up to the prediction point: the last **≤14 turns**, each truncated to ~200 words, labeled `[DEVELOPER]` / `[AGENT]`. This contains the real user's earlier messages and the real agent's replies up to that moment.

4. **The task instruction** — "Write the developer's *next* message… output only the literal text they'd type, no commentary."

So the prompt skeleton is:

```
<user_profile> …distilled folder… </user_profile>     ← input 1 (distilled only)
You are role-playing the developer described above.
…using an AI coding agent in `{repo}`.                 ← input 2
<conversation> [DEVELOPER]:… [AGENT]:… (≤14 turns) </conversation>   ← input 3
Write the developer's NEXT message. Output ONLY the text…            ← input 4
```

**What it does *not* get** (deliberately): the real next message (that's the held-out answer), anything from the future of the session, and — in v0's inline setup — the actual repo files. The simulator also chooses its **own move** here; nothing tells it whether to approve, push back, etc.

**Optional inputs that exist in the fuller simulator but weren't used for this frontier-model run:** the richer `build_folder_prompt` path (used by the repo's v5 / closed-loop scripts) can also hand the simulator (a) the shared `simulator/AGENT.md` playbook, (b) a **live checkout of the repo** to read via Read/Glob/Grep, and (c) a **forced move** sampled from the user's move-mix prior. v0 left those off so the three frontier models were compared on the clean, model-agnostic inline prompt.

Mapping back to the earlier formalization: input 1 = `c_persona`, input 3 = `h_{<t}` (history), with `c_intent`/`c_evidence` (hidden goal, evidence pool) deliberately *not* supplied in v0 — that's the realistic, no-leakage setting.

content.content:
# Page Description: SWE-chat Analysis

## Project Overview
This page presents quantitative analysis of SALT-NLP/SWE-chat, a dataset containing real coding-agent sessions from multiple developers working across numerous repositories with Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI.

## Main Sections and Headings

1. **"What does the human actually contribute?"** — Opening context about the dataset scope
2. **"The user's footprint"** — Analysis of genuine human-typed messages
3. **"Who fills the context window?"** — Comparison of turn types and token distribution
4. **"Abnormally long user messages"** — Heavy-tailed distribution analysis with visualization
5. **"Explore"** — Navigation links to related tools

## Key Metrics Presented

**Dataset Scale:**
- 2,692,480 conversation turn-rows
- 5,851 coding sessions from 187 developers across 205 repositories
- 304M content tokens total

**User Contribution:**
- 62,544 user messages (56,842 distinct)
- 23.1M user tokens
- Median of 32 tokens per message; mean of 369.8

**Token Distribution:**
- User messages: 7.6% of total tokens
- Assistant: 2.8%
- Tool operations: 89.6%
- Tool turns outnumber user messages by 12.2×

**Message Length Extremes:**
- Longest message: 475,827 tokens (error log paste)
- Top 1% of messages contain 30.3% of all user tokens
- Messages ≥1,000 tokens represent 8.7% of messages but 73.5% of tokens

## Visual Elements
A logarithmic cumulative distribution graph shows message length distribution with dual-line visualization comparing message count percentage against token percentage.

## Methodology Note
Token counts use tiktoken o200k_base; analysis derives from conversations.parquet source data.

1	# User Simulator for `with-user` — Deliverables
2	
3	End-to-end pipeline to train a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct user simulator (LoRA SFT)
4	on one SWE-chat user's data, served on Modal as an OpenAI-compatible endpoint
5	that drops into [`akhatua2/with-user`](https://github.com/akhatua2/with-user).
6	
7	## What was built
8	
9	### 1. Research (`notes/`)
10	- **literature.md** — survey of recent user-simulator training (UserLM-8b
11	  Microsoft 2025, DAUS, USS, DIAL) and the recipe choice.
12	- **dataset.md** — SWE-chat schema, user picks (chose `marcus-sa`: 450 raw
13	  sessions → 261 usable after filtering Conductor wrappers, slash-command
14	  stubs, and short conversations).
15	- **integration.md** — the *exact* integration spec for with-user.
16	- **with-user-prompts.md** — verbatim system / initial / step Jinja2 templates
17	  with the JSON output schema.
18	
19	### 2. Pipeline (`modal_app/`)
20	All heavy state lives on Modal Volume V2 `user-sim-vol`; local workdir is
21	~150 KB of scripts.
22	
23	| File | What it does |
24	|---|---|
25	| `common.py` | Shared Modal app, volume, and 3 images (data / train / serve). |
26	| `data_prep.py` | Loads `SALT-NLP/SWE-chat`, filters to one user, splits long sessions into 10-step chunks, builds `(system, initial, step, decision)` SFT examples. Saves JSONL to `/vol/data/<user_id>/`. |
27	| `train.py` | Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct + LoRA r=64 SFT on A100-80GB. Merges and saves to `/vol/ckpt/<run>/merged/`. |
28	| `serve.py` | vLLM OpenAI-compatible web server (L40S GPU, scales to 0). |
29	| `smoke_test.py` | Hits the deployed endpoint with two real with-user-style messages and parses the JSON. |
30	| `eval.py` | Loads the merged checkpoint and reports decision-accuracy on val. |
31	| `inspect_data.py` | Spot-check a prepared training example. |
32	
33	### 3. Drop-in artifact (`configs/`)
34	`configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml` — copy into `with-user/` to override the
35	user-sim model and point at the Modal endpoint.
36	
37	## Quickstart
38	
39	```bash
40	# 0. Auth (one-time)
41	modal token set --token-id ... --token-secret ...
42	modal profile activate marl
43	modal secret create huggingface HF_TOKEN=hf_xxx
44	
45	# 1. Prepare data (~2 min)
46	cd modal_app && modal run data_prep.py --user-id marcus-sa
47	
48	# 2. Train (~2.5h on A100-80GB, ~$6 of credits)
49	modal run --detach train.py --user-id marcus-sa
50	
51	# 3. Serve
52	modal deploy serve.py
53	# → URL: https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run
54	
55	# 4. Smoke test
56	python smoke_test.py --base-url https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run/v1
57	```
58	
59	## Drop into with-user
60	
61	```bash
62	git clone https://github.com/akhatua2/with-user
63	cp ../with-user-pkg/configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml configs/
64	
65	export OPENAI_API_BASE="https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run/v1"
66	export OPENAI_API_KEY="user-sim-dev-key"
67	
68	with-user run \
69	  --benchmark swebench --harness mini-swe-agent \
70	  --subset verified --split test --slice 0:5 \
71	  --model openai/gpt-5 \
72	  -c configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml \
73	  -w 5 -o runs/usersim-marcus-sa
74	```
75	
76	## Key design choices
77	
78	1. **Base model = Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (not Qwen3-8B).** Qwen3 has a thinking
79	   mode (`<think>` blocks) that complicates pure-JSON output. Qwen2.5 has the
80	   same Apache-2.0 license, 32K context, strong code understanding, and is
81	   well-supported by vLLM. Both fit the under-8B budget.
82	2. **LoRA, not full FT.** r=64 on all attention + MLP projections, trainable
83	   params 2.08% of total (161M). Per the Thinking Machines "LoRA Without
84	   Regret" study, this matches full-FT quality on small datasets while
85	   keeping the checkpoint small and training cheap.
86	3. **One example per session, chunked.** Sessions have median 50+ events;
87	   we chunk into 10-step windows (~4K tokens each) so multi-turn context is
88	   preserved without truncation.
89	4. **Decision construction from raw data.** For each assistant action in the
90	   real conversation, we look ahead: if a user_prompt followed before the next
91	   assistant action, the target is `interject` with that prompt; otherwise
92	   `continue`. Final distribution: 15% interject / 85% continue, matching
93	   the system prompt's "prefer silent" guidance.
94	5. **Full-sequence loss (no assistant-only mask).** TRL 0.12.1 doesn't
95	   support `assistant_only_loss`, and Qwen2.5's default chat template has no
96	   `{% generation %}` marker. We accept slight loss-mask suboptimality; the
97	   model still emits well-formatted JSON because the system prompt and
98	   training data both reinforce it.
99	6. **vLLM on L40S, not A100.** L40S has 48 GB which fits Qwen2.5-7B bf16 +
100	   reasonable KV cache, at ~$1.95/h vs $2.50/h for A100.
101	7. **Volume V2 for everything heavy.** HF cache, processed datasets, model
102	   checkpoints all live on `user-sim-vol`. Local workdir is just scripts.
103	8. **Avoid `claude/anthropic/sonnet/opus/haiku` in the served name** —
104	   `with-user/src/withuser/user_sim.py:104` would otherwise inject Anthropic
105	   cache-control blocks. The endpoint serves `user-sim`, which is safe.
106	
107	## Budget
108	
109	| Item | $ |
110	|---|---|
111	| Data prep (CPU, ~2 min) | $0.05 |
112	| Training (A100-80GB, ~2.5h, includes image build) | $7 |
113	| Serving (L40S, ~1h smoke test) | $2 |
114	| **Total estimate** | **~$10** |
115	
116	Well under the $30 cap.
117

file.content:
# User Simulator for `with-user` — Deliverables

End-to-end pipeline to train a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct user simulator (LoRA SFT)
on one SWE-chat user's data, served on Modal as an OpenAI-compatible endpoint
that drops into [`akhatua2/with-user`](https://github.com/akhatua2/with-user).

## What was built

### 1. Research (`notes/`)
- **literature.md** — survey of recent user-simulator training (UserLM-8b
  Microsoft 2025, DAUS, USS, DIAL) and the recipe choice.
- **dataset.md** — SWE-chat schema, user picks (chose `marcus-sa`: 450 raw
  sessions → 261 usable after filtering Conductor wrappers, slash-command
  stubs, and short conversations).
- **integration.md** — the *exact* integration spec for with-user.
- **with-user-prompts.md** — verbatim system / initial / step Jinja2 templates
  with the JSON output schema.

### 2. Pipeline (`modal_app/`)
All heavy state lives on Modal Volume V2 `user-sim-vol`; local workdir is
~150 KB of scripts.

| File | What it does |
|---|---|
| `common.py` | Shared Modal app, volume, and 3 images (data / train / serve). |
| `data_prep.py` | Loads `SALT-NLP/SWE-chat`, filters to one user, splits long sessions into 10-step chunks, builds `(system, initial, step, decision)` SFT examples. Saves JSONL to `/vol/data/<user_id>/`. |
| `train.py` | Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct + LoRA r=64 SFT on A100-80GB. Merges and saves to `/vol/ckpt/<run>/merged/`. |
| `serve.py` | vLLM OpenAI-compatible web server (L40S GPU, scales to 0). |
| `smoke_test.py` | Hits the deployed endpoint with two real with-user-style messages and parses the JSON. |
| `eval.py` | Loads the merged checkpoint and reports decision-accuracy on val. |
| `inspect_data.py` | Spot-check a prepared training example. |

### 3. Drop-in artifact (`configs/`)
`configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml` — copy into `with-user/` to override the
user-sim model and point at the Modal endpoint.

## Quickstart

```bash
# 0. Auth (one-time)
modal token set --token-id ... --token-secret ...
modal profile activate marl
modal secret create huggingface HF_TOKEN=hf_xxx

# 1. Prepare data (~2 min)
cd modal_app && modal run data_prep.py --user-id marcus-sa

# 2. Train (~2.5h on A100-80GB, ~$6 of credits)
modal run --detach train.py --user-id marcus-sa

# 3. Serve
modal deploy serve.py
# → URL: https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run

# 4. Smoke test
python smoke_test.py --base-url https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run/v1
```

## Drop into with-user

```bash
git clone https://github.com/akhatua2/with-user
cp ../with-user-pkg/configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml configs/

export OPENAI_API_BASE="https://<workspace>--user-sim-marcus-sa-serve.modal.run/v1"
export OPENAI_API_KEY="user-sim-dev-key"

with-user run \
  --benchmark swebench --harness mini-swe-agent \
  --subset verified --split test --slice 0:5 \
  --model openai/gpt-5 \
  -c configs/user-sim-marcus-sa.yaml \
  -w 5 -o runs/usersim-marcus-sa
```

## Key design choices

1. **Base model = Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (not Qwen3-8B).** Qwen3 has a thinking
   mode (`<think>` blocks) that complicates pure-JSON output. Qwen2.5 has the
   same Apache-2.0 license, 32K context, strong code understanding, and is
   well-supported by vLLM. Both fit the under-8B budget.
2. **LoRA, not full FT.** r=64 on all attention + MLP projections, trainable
   params 2.08% of total (161M). Per the Thinking Machines "LoRA Without
   Regret" study, this matches full-FT quality on small datasets while
   keeping the checkpoint small and training cheap.
3. **One example per session, chunked.** Sessions have median 50+ events;
   we chunk into 10-step windows (~4K tokens each) so multi-turn context is
   preserved without truncation.
4. **Decision construction from raw data.** For each assistant action in the
   real conversation, we look ahead: if a user_prompt followed before the next
   assistant action, the target is `interject` with that prompt; otherwise
   `continue`. Final distribution: 15% interject / 85% continue, matching
   the system prompt's "prefer silent" guidance.
5. **Full-sequence loss (no assistant-only mask).** TRL 0.12.1 doesn't
   support `assistant_only_loss`, and Qwen2.5's default chat template has no
   `{% generation %}` marker. We accept slight loss-mask suboptimality; the
   model still emits well-formatted JSON because the system prompt and
   training data both reinforce it.
6. **vLLM on L40S, not A100.** L40S has 48 GB which fits Qwen2.5-7B bf16 +
   reasonable KV cache, at ~$1.95/h vs $2.50/h for A100.
7. **Volume V2 for everything heavy.** HF cache, processed datasets, model
   checkpoints all live on `user-sim-vol`. Local workdir is just scripts.
8. **Avoid `claude/anthropic/sonnet/opus/haiku` in the served name** —
   `with-user/src/withuser/user_sim.py:104` would otherwise inject Anthropic
   cache-control blocks. The endpoint serves `user-sim`, which is safe.

## Budget

| Item | $ |
|---|---|
| Data prep (CPU, ~2 min) | $0.05 |
| Training (A100-80GB, ~2.5h, includes image build) | $7 |
| Serving (L40S, ~1h smoke test) | $2 |
| **Total estimate** | **~$10** |

Well under the $30 cap.

content.text: Backup branch pushed (`backup/trace-viewer-pre-usersimbench`). Now, the user's key emphasis is making the confusing metrics crystal-clear for a SWE-chat-literate but user-sim-naive reader. I'll run a focused workflow to draft and adversarially refine accessible copy, grounded strictly in the exact numbers, then implement the page.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'usersimbench-site-copy',
  description: 'Draft + adversarially refine accessible website copy for the v0 UserSimBench findings (audience: knows SWE-chat, new to user simulation; metrics must be crystal clear, no scary formulas)',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Draft', detail: 'metric explainers + narrative + page/viz spec in parallel' },
    { title: 'Critique', detail: 'accuracy + accessibility critique vs ground-truth numbers' },
    { title: 'Synthesize', detail: 'one implementation-ready copy doc' },
  ],
}

const AUDIENCE = `AUDIENCE: a reader who knows the SWE-chat dataset well (real Claude-Code coding sessions: users prompt, the agent edits code/runs tools, users push back / report bugs / approve) but has LITTLE idea what a "user simulator" is or why anyone evaluates one. Tone: concise, plain, confident, no academic hedging, no buzzwords. NO scary formulas in the body — if a formula must appear, hide it behind a plain-language sentence and a tiny worked example first. Define "user simulator" in one sentence the first time it appears.`

const GROUND_TRUTH = `
GROUND-TRUTH NUMBERS — use ONLY these; never invent or recompute differently.

WHAT THE BENCHMARK DOES (v0, "UserSimBench"):
- A user simulator = an AI pretending to be the human developer, so we can test coding agents without a real person in the loop.
- We take 54 real held-out moments from 9 real SWE-chat developers. At each moment we show a candidate simulator the real conversation so far (the real agent's work is held fixed) and ask it to write the developer's next message.
- We label that message's MOVE with a 7-way classifier (the repo's speech-act classifier, run by claude-haiku-4.5). The 7 moves: approve_proceed, new_work, refine_redirect, bug_report, pushback, interrupt, question (+ "other").
- We compare the simulator's moves to what the real developer actually did. It scores the MOVE, not the exact words, because exact-word fidelity was already shown to be saturated (a real message and a simulated one are about equally "realistic" to a judge; one message barely identifies a person).

REAL DEVELOPERS (the target, n=54):
- move mix: approve 24%, new_work 20%, refine_redirect 15%, bug_report 13%, pushback 11%, interrupt 9%, question 6%.
- approve_proceed rate = 24% (about 1 in 4 turns is "looks good / continue").
- "critical" rate (pushback + interrupt + bug_report) = 33% (about 1 in 3 turns the developer says something is wrong).

THE METRICS (these are the ones that were presented confusingly and must be made crystal clear):
1. MoveFid (0-100): how well the simulator's overall MIX of move-types matches real developers' mix. 100 = identical mix. (It is a per-move overlap averaged across the 7 moves — Sørensen-Dice — but say it in plain words: "do they push back / approve / report bugs in the same proportions as real devs?")
2. CondAgree (0-1): of the 54 moments, the fraction where the simulator made the SAME move the real developer made AT THAT moment. This is harder than matching the mix — it's right-move-right-place.
3. Marginal ceiling = 0.163 (Sigma p-squared): the CondAgree you'd get by GUESSING from the mix alone with zero understanding of the moment (just drawing a random move weighted by how common each move is). So CondAgree above 0.163 = the simulator has real situational skill, not just the right averages. Explain it as "the lucky-guess line."
4. approve% and critical%: the simulator's own rate of approving vs being critical. Comparing these to the real 24%/33% is the EASY-MODE tell.
5. easy-mode: simulators that are too agreeable — they approve too much and rarely push back — making the coding agent look better than real users would. This is the central failure.

RESULTS — distilled = given a profile of the specific real developer; generic = no profile.
| simulator | MoveFid | CondAgree | approve% | critical% |
| deepseek-v3.1 / distilled | 53.9 | 0.315 | 42.6% | 9.3% |
| deepseek-v3.1 / generic   | 39.8 | 0.189 | 49.1% | 5.7% |
| gpt-5 / distilled         | 45.5 | 0.278 | 38.9% | 3.7% |
| gpt-5 / generic           | 52.4 | 0.189 | 24.5% | 11.3% |
| gemini-3.1-pro / distilled| 41.4 | 0.333 | 72.2% | 3.7% |
| gemini-3.1-pro / generic  | 49.0 | 0.278 | 66.7% | 11.1% |
| [ref] prior_sampler       | 84.3 | 0.185 | 20.4% | 35.2% |
| [ref] always_approve      | 4.9  | 0.241 | 100%  | 0% |

KEY FACTS / FINDINGS (all derivable from above; state them plainly):
- Easy-mode is universal: every model approves more than the real 24% (38.9%-72.2%) and is critical far less than the real 33% (only 3.7%-11.3%). Simulators reproduce only ~11%-34% of the real critical-feedback rate.
- prior_sampler is a DUMB baseline that just samples moves from the real mix. It TOPS MoveFid (84.3) — proving MoveFid alone is gameable: matching the average mix is easy. But its CondAgree (0.185) is basically the lucky-guess line (0.163) — it has no situational skill. Lesson: judge a simulator by the PAIR (does it match the mix AND beat the lucky-guess line AND keep approve/critical near real), never by one number.
- The real frontier models DO beat the lucky-guess line on CondAgree (distilled: 0.278-0.333 ≈ 2x the 0.163 line) — they have genuine situational skill — but they score LOWER MoveFid than the dumb sampler because they skew toward approval.
- The persona profile ("distilled") raises CondAgree for all three models (deepseek +0.126, gpt-5 +0.089, gemini +0.055) — it helps them pick the right move at the right moment. But it does NOT fix easy-mode; for gpt-5 and gemini it makes them approve EVEN MORE.
- Transfer from prior user-sim research (which tested these models on customer-service chat, not coding):
  * DeepSeek-V3.1 was the BEST simulator in prior work (a score called USI = 76.0). Here it transfers as the most COACHABLE: biggest gain from the persona profile, top distilled MoveFid (53.9).
  * GPT-5 was strong-but-not-best in prior work (USI ~70.9; prior finding: "a stronger model is not automatically a better simulator"). Here, uninstructed GPT-5 has the best-calibrated approve rate (24.5% ≈ real 24%) yet still barely pushes back.
  * Gemini-3.1-Pro was rated the MOST HUMAN-LIKE model in prior work (on a raw-text human-likeness probe). Here it is the WORST easy-mode offender (approves 67-72%, ~3x real). Sounding human != behaving like a real developer. This is the sharpest transfer result.
- One-line takeaway: today's top models are too polite to stand in for real developers — they rarely push back the way real people do — and being "good at sounding human" or "good at simulating customers" does NOT mean good at simulating a coder.

CAVEATS: 54 moments, 9 users, one generation per cell, a single (Haiku) move-classifier, and this is "request-side" only (we held the real agent fixed; we did not yet let the simulator drive a live agent and measure task outcomes). Treat tiny gaps as noise; the easy-mode effect is large.
`

phase('Draft')

const CARD_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object', additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    cards: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object', additionalProperties: false,
        properties: {
          metric: { type: 'string', description: 'the technical name, e.g. MoveFid' },
          plain_name: { type: 'string', description: 'a friendly label a newcomer gets instantly' },
          one_liner: { type: 'string', description: 'one sentence, no jargon, no formula' },
          why_it_matters: { type: 'string' },
          worked_example: { type: 'string', description: 'a tiny concrete example with real numbers from the ground truth' },
          gotcha: { type: 'string', description: 'the trap / how it can mislead (e.g. MoveFid gameable)' },
        },
        required: ['metric', 'plain_name', 'one_liner', 'why_it_matters', 'worked_example', 'gotcha'],
      },
    },
  },
  required: ['cards'],
}

const [cards, narrative, viz] = await parallel([
  () => agent(`${AUDIENCE}\n\nWrite crystal-clear explainer cards for EACH of these metrics: MoveFid, CondAgree, the marginal/lucky-guess ceiling (0.163), approve% & critical%, and "easy-mode". Each card: a friendly plain_name, a one-sentence jargon-free definition, why it matters, a tiny worked example using the real ground-truth numbers, and the gotcha (how the metric can mislead). The whole point is to DEFUSE scary formulas — turn Sørensen-Dice and Sigma-p-squared into plain intuition.\n\n${GROUND_TRUTH}`,
    { label: 'metric-cards', phase: 'Draft', schema: CARD_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`${AUDIENCE}\n\nWrite the website NARRATIVE COPY (prose, ~450-650 words) for a single-page results site titled around "Are AI models good enough to stand in for real developers?". Structure: (1) a 1-2 sentence hook + a one-sentence definition of "user simulator" grounded in SWE-chat; (2) what we tested and why moves-not-words; (3) the headline finding = easy-mode / too-polite, with the real 24%/33% vs simulator numbers; (4) the "don't trust one number" insight (dumb prior_sampler tops MoveFid); (5) the transfer story across the 3 models (DeepSeek=coachable, GPT-5=calibrated-but-passive, Gemini=human-sounding-but-worst); (6) a crisp one-line bottom line; (7) one short caveats sentence. Use ONLY ground-truth numbers. Section headings should be plain-English questions/statements a SWE-chat reader would click.\n\n${GROUND_TRUTH}`,
    { label: 'narrative', phase: 'Draft', effort: 'high' }),

  () => agent(`You are a data-viz + information-architecture designer for a clean, minimal, light-theme (Tailwind, zinc/blue/emerald/amber, mono for numbers) single-page static site. Propose the PAGE LAYOUT and the specific VISUALS to render the v0 results, with EXACT data bindings to the ground-truth numbers. Prioritize: (a) a hero stat that lands the easy-mode point instantly; (b) a real-vs-simulator MOVE-MIX comparison (esp. approve% and critical% bars, real baseline marked); (c) the leaderboard table with the key columns and the references row; (d) a visual that shows "MoveFid high but CondAgree at the lucky-guess line" for the dumb sampler. For each visual: what it shows, why, the exact numbers/series, and the plain-English caption. Keep it implementable as simple divs/SVG bars (no chart lib). Specify section order.\n\n${GROUND_TRUTH}`,
    { label: 'viz-spec', phase: 'Draft', effort: 'high' }),
])

phase('Critique')

const CRIT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object', additionalProperties: false,
  properties: {
    number_errors: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'any stat that contradicts ground truth' },
    remaining_jargon: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'words/phrases a user-sim newcomer would not get' },
    clarity_fixes: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    overclaims: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'claims stronger than n=54 / the data supports' },
    missing: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    keep: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'what is working and must be preserved' },
  },
  required: ['number_errors', 'remaining_jargon', 'clarity_fixes', 'overclaims', 'missing', 'keep'],
}

const critique = await agent(`You are a ruthless editor checking the draft site copy below for (a) ANY number that contradicts the ground truth, (b) jargon a SWE-chat reader new to user simulation would not understand, (c) overclaims given only 54 moments / 9 users, (d) clarity problems, (e) anything missing. Be specific and quote the offending text.\n\n=== METRIC CARDS ===\n${JSON.stringify(cards, null, 1)}\n\n=== NARRATIVE ===\n${narrative}\n\n=== VIZ SPEC ===\n${viz}\n\n${GROUND_TRUTH}`,
  { label: 'critique', phase: 'Critique', schema: CRIT_SCHEMA, effort: 'high' })

phase('Synthesize')

const final = await agent(`You are the lead writer. Produce the FINAL, implementation-ready copy document for the single-page static site, resolving every issue in the critique and using ONLY ground-truth numbers. A front-end engineer will build a Tailwind/Next.js page directly from this — so be concrete and complete.

Output clean markdown with these labeled sections:
1. PAGE TITLE + META DESCRIPTION + HERO (the one stat/sentence that lands easy-mode instantly).
2. NARRATIVE — the final prose with plain-English section headings (incorporate the metric intuition inline so the page reads top-to-bottom without prerequisites).
3. METRIC EXPLAINERS — the final card text for MoveFid, CondAgree, lucky-guess line, approve%/critical%, easy-mode (each: plain name, one-liner, why, tiny example, gotcha).
4. LEADERBOARD TABLE — exact rows/columns to render (models + references), and the plain-English note under it.
5. VISUALS — the final list of visuals with exact data series, plain captions, and section order, implementable as simple divs/SVG bars.
6. CAVEATS — the final one short paragraph.
Make every number match the ground truth exactly. Keep it tight and readable.

=== DRAFTS ===\nCARDS:\n${JSON.stringify(cards, null, 1)}\n\nNARRATIVE:\n${narrative}\n\nVIZ:\n${viz}\n\n=== CRITIQUE TO RESOLVE ===\n${JSON.stringify(critique, null, 1)}\n\n${GROUND_TRUTH}`,
  { label: 'synthesis', phase: 'Synthesize', effort: 'high' })

return { final, critique, cards, narrative, viz }

input.command:
cd /Users/kevin/Dev/user-simulator/web
# 1. transform the exact v0 numbers into a clean frontend data file
python3 - <<'PY'
import json
s=json.load(open('/Users/kevin/Dev/user.skill/bench/results/v0_summary.json'))
R=s['results']
prior={
 "deepseek-v3.1":"Best user simulator in prior work (customer-service USI 76.0)",
 "gpt-5":"Strong but not best in prior work (USI ~70.9); 'stronger ≠ better simulator'",
 "gemini-3.1-pro":"Rated most human-like model in prior work (raw-text probe)",
}
labels={"deepseek-v3.1":"DeepSeek-V3.1","gpt-5":"GPT-5","gemini-3.1-pro":"Gemini-3.1-Pro"}
def row(key,label,cond,ref=False,note=None):
    r=R[key]
    return {"id":key,"label":label,"cond":cond,"ref":ref,"note":note,
            "moveFid":r["MoveFid"],"condAgree":r["CondAgree"],
            "approve":r["approve%"],"critical":r["critical%"],"active":r["active%"]}
rows=[]
for m,lab in labels.items():
    rows.append(row(f"{m}/distilled",lab,"distilled",note=prior[m]))
    rows.append(row(f"{m}/generic",lab,"generic",note=prior[m]))
rows.append(row("[ref] prior_sampler","Prior-sampler (dumb baseline)","—",ref=True,
                note="Just samples moves from the real mix — no understanding of the moment"))
rows.append(row("[ref] always_approve","Always-approve bot","—",ref=True,
                note="Says 'looks good' every turn — the easy-mode worst case"))
out={
 "nPoints":s["n_points"],"nUsers":9,
 "real":{"approve":s["real_approve%"],"critical":s["real_critical%"],
         "moveMix":s["real_move_distribution"]},
 "luckyGuess":s["condagree_marginal_ceiling"],
 "judge":s["judge"],
 "rows":rows,
}
json.dump(out,open('public/data/v0_results.json','w'),indent=1)
print("wrote public/data/v0_results.json")
print(json.dumps(out,indent=1)[:500])
PY
# 2. clear old site (preserved in backup branch)
rm -rf app/patch-similarity app/swe-chat-sessions public/traces
rm -f public/data/agent_judge_compare.json public/data/agent_judge_pilot.json public/data/patch_similarity_reverse.json public/data/patch_similarity.json public/data/swe_chat_sessions.json
echo "=== remaining web tree ==="
find . -type f -not -path '*/node_modules/*' -not -path '*/.next/*' -not -path '*/out/*' | grep -vE 'package-lock' | sort
