# UserSimBench v0 — Final Copy Document

---

## 1. PAGE TITLE + META DESCRIPTION + HERO

**Page title (`<title>`):**
Are AI Models Good Enough to Stand In for Real Developers? — UserSimBench v0

**Meta description:**
We tested whether today's top AI models can role-play a real software developer to stress-test coding agents. They can't: every model is too polite. Real developers say "something's wrong" on 1 turn in 3; the toughest AI model manages 1 in 9.

**Hero:**

> ### Every AI model we tested is stuck in "easy mode."
>
> **33%** — real developers push back, interrupt, or report a bug on about one turn in three.
> **11.3%** — the most critical AI model (excluding the dumb baseline) manages only that.
>
> A *user simulator* is an AI pretending to be the human developer, so we can stress-test coding agents without a real person in the loop. The catch: every model we tested is too agreeable. They approve more than real developers (real 24% → 39–72%) and complain far less (real 33% → 3.7–11.3%) — so any coding agent they test looks better than real users would ever let it.

**Hero mini-bar** (two horizontal SVG bars, shared scale 0–40%):
- Bar A — "Real developers, critical%": value `33%`, emerald, width = 33/40.
- Bar B — "Toughest AI model, critical%": value `11.3%` (gpt-5, no profile), amber, width = 11.3/40.

**Hero caption:**
Real developers say *something's wrong* on one turn in three. The most critical AI model does it on roughly one in nine. The rest are gentler still. (The only thing that reaches the real rate is a dumb random baseline — more on that below.)

---

## 2. NARRATIVE

### Are AI models good enough to stand in for real developers?

When we test a coding agent, we usually need a human on the other end — someone to ask for a feature, complain when the diff is wrong, and say "ship it" when it's right. A **user simulator** is an AI that plays that human developer for us, so we can stress-test coding agents at scale without a real person in the loop. The question this page answers is simple: are today's best models actually good enough to do that job? The short answer is no — and the reason is more interesting than "they're not smart enough."

### What we tested, and why we grade the move instead of the words

We took **54 conversation moments** from **9 real developers** chatting with coding agents. (These are *held-out* moments — set aside as a test set and never shown to any model during tuning.) At each moment we showed a candidate simulator the real conversation so far — with the real agent's work held fixed — and asked it to write the developer's next message.

Then we labeled what that message was *trying to do* — its **move**. A move is just the labeled intent of a message. There are seven: **approve** ("looks good, continue"), **new work**, **refine/redirect**, **bug report**, **pushback**, **interrupt**, and **question** (plus a catch-all "other"). The labeling was done automatically by a single AI classifier (claude-haiku-4.5), so its mistakes set a floor on how precise every number below can be.

We grade the move, not the exact wording. Prior work found word-level realism largely saturated — a judge can barely tell a real message from a simulated one, and a single line of text hardly identifies a person. What actually matters is behavior: does the simulator push back, approve, and report bugs *in the same situations* a real developer would?

A note on counts: every percentage below is out of **54 moments**, so the sparse moves rest on a handful of examples (11% pushback ≈ 6 turns). Read small gaps as noise; read the big, repeated patterns as real.

### The headline: every model is too polite

Real developers approve about **24%** of the time (roughly 1 turn in 4) and say *something is wrong* — pushback, interrupt, or bug report — about **33%** of the time (1 turn in 3). Every live model we tested is too agreeable. They approve between **39% and 72%** of the time, and they're critical only **3.7% to 11.3%** of the time — reproducing only about **a tenth to a third** of real developers' critical-feedback rate. A coding agent tested against these simulators looks better than it would against real users, because the simulated developer rarely complains. We call this **easy mode**, and it's the central failure on this page.

### Don't trust one number

We use two main scores. **MoveFid** (0–100) asks whether the simulator's overall *mix* of moves matches real developers — do they approve, push back, and report bugs in the same proportions? 100 means an identical mix. **CondAgree** (0–1) is the harder test: across the 54 moments, how often did the simulator make the *same move the real developer made, right there*? Right move, right place.

Here's the trap. A dumb baseline called `prior_sampler` — it just draws moves at random from the real mix, with zero understanding of any moment — **tops MoveFid at 84.3**, beating every real model. Matching the average is easy. But its CondAgree is **0.185**, basically the **lucky-guess line** (0.163 — the score you'd get from blind weighted guessing). It has no situational skill at all. The lesson: judge a simulator by the whole picture — match the mix, beat the lucky-guess line, *and* keep approve/critical rates near reality — never by one number.

The frontier models, **with a developer profile**, do clear the situational-skill bar: the three with-profile runs land at CondAgree **0.278–0.333**, roughly **1.7× to 2×** the lucky-guess line. They genuinely read the moment. (A caution on what CondAgree alone proves: even a brain-dead "always approve" baseline scores 0.241 — above the line — just because approve is the single most common move. So beating the line is necessary, not sufficient.) These models simply score lower MoveFid than the dumb sampler, because they lean too hard on approval.

### How the three models carry over from earlier work

Prior research tested these same models as *customer-service* simulators, not coding ones, using a combined quality score (USI). The results don't carry over the way you'd expect.

- **DeepSeek-V3.1** was the best simulator in that prior work (USI 76.0). Here it's the most *coachable*: a developer profile lifts its CondAgree the most (+0.126, from 0.189 to 0.315) and it earns the top with-profile MoveFid (53.9).
- **GPT-5** was strong-but-not-best before (USI ≈ 70.9; the prior finding was that a stronger model isn't automatically a better simulator). Here, with no profile, it has the best-calibrated approve rate (24.5%, almost exactly the real 24%) — yet it still barely pushes back (critical 11.3%). Note: that calibration is the *no-profile* GPT-5 only; with a profile it jumps to 38.9% approval.
- **Gemini-3.1-Pro** was rated the most human-*sounding* model in prior work. Here it's the worst easy-mode offender, approving 67–72% of the time, about 3× real developers. Sounding human is not the same as behaving like one — the sharpest result on this page. (With one run per cell, treat the exact ranking loosely; the direction is unmistakable.)

Adding a developer profile helps all three pick the right move at the right moment, but it does **not** fix easy mode — for GPT-5 and Gemini it actually pushes them to approve *even more* (24.5→38.9%, 66.7→72.2%).

### Bottom line

Today's top models are too polite to stand in for real developers: they rarely push back the way real people do. And being good at *sounding human* — or good at simulating *customers* — does not make a model good at simulating a *coder*.

---

## 3. METRIC EXPLAINERS

## 3. METRIC EXPLAINERS

> **Shared anchor to render once, above the cards (zinc-500):** A **move** is the labeled intent of a developer's message — one of seven: approve, new work, refine/redirect, bug report, pushback, interrupt, question. Throughout, **with-profile** means the simulator was given a profile of the specific real developer; **no-profile** means it wasn't.

---

**Card 1 — MoveFid**
- **Plain name:** Right-mix score — does the simulator have the right move-blend?
- **One-liner:** How closely the simulator's overall blend of move-types (approve, push back, report bugs, ask questions…) matches the blend real developers produce. 100 = identical blend.
- **Why it matters:** If the simulator never reports bugs or never pushes back, the coding agent only ever gets tested on easy days. MoveFid checks the diet: across many turns, does the simulator approve, redirect, and complain in roughly the same proportions as real people?
- **Tiny example:** Real developers approve 24%, do new work 20%, refine/redirect 15%, report bugs 13%, push back 11%, interrupt 9%, ask questions 6%. A simulator that hits those same percentages scores high.
- **Gotcha:** MoveFid only looks at the *overall* blend, not whether each move lands at the right moment — so it's gameable. The brainless `prior_sampler`, which literally draws moves from the real mix, **tops the chart at 84.3** with zero situational skill. A high MoveFid alone tells you nothing about whether the simulator pushes back when the code is actually broken.

---

**Card 2 — CondAgree**
- **Plain name:** Right-move-right-place score
- **One-liner:** Of the 54 real moments, the fraction where the simulator made the very same move the real developer made at that exact moment. (Scale 0–1.)
- **Why it matters:** Matching the average blend is cheap; reacting correctly turn-by-turn is the real test. CondAgree asks the harder question — at this specific point in this specific conversation, did the simulator do what the real human did?
- **Tiny example:** Gemini-3.1-Pro with a developer profile scores 0.333 — it picked the real developer's exact move at about a third of the 54 moments. DeepSeek-V3.1 with-profile scores 0.315; the dumb `prior_sampler` scores only 0.185.
- **Gotcha:** A third sounds low, but moves are a 7-way choice, so the bar isn't 50% — it's the lucky-guess line of 0.163 (next card). And scoring well here doesn't mean the simulator is well-behaved: Gemini has the highest CondAgree yet approves 72% of the time — right-move-right-place *and* badly easy-mode at once. (With 54 moments and one run per cell, "highest" means leading, not provably best.)

---

**Card 3 — The lucky-guess line (0.163)**
- **Plain name:** The lucky-guess line *(formula name, small/muted: marginal ceiling, Σp²)*
- **One-liner:** The CondAgree you'd get by blindly guessing moves from the real mix without understanding the moment at all — so anything clearly above it means real situational skill.
- **Why it matters:** Without a reference point, a CondAgree number is meaningless. This is that reference: the score you get purely by luck if you know the right average proportions but read nothing about what's happening. Beating 0.163 is the proof a simulator is actually reading the situation.
- **Tiny example:** Guess by the real mix and you'll say "approve" about 24% of the time, while the real dev also approved about 24% — so you accidentally agree on approve about 0.24 × 0.24 of the time. Add up that lucky overlap across all seven moves and it comes to 0.163. The dumb `prior_sampler` scores 0.185 — sitting on the line. The with-profile frontier runs hit 0.278–0.333, about 1.7× to 2× the line.
- **Gotcha:** Clearing the line by a hair is noise, not skill (only 54 moments, one run per cell). And a simulator can beat the line on situational accuracy while still being badly miscalibrated on how often it approves or complains — the line says "has real skill," not "behaves like a real developer." (Even a constant "always approve" baseline clears it, at 0.241.)

---

**Card 4 — approve% and critical%**
- **Plain name:** The temperament check — how nice vs. how tough?
- **One-liner:** The simulator's own rate of saying "looks good, continue" (approve%) versus its rate of saying something's wrong — pushback + interrupt + bug report (critical%) — measured against real developers' **24%** and **33%**.
- **Why it matters:** This is the quickest read on whether a simulator behaves like a real developer or like a pushover. Real developers approve about 1 turn in 4 and object about 1 turn in 3. A simulator that approves far more and objects far less makes any coding agent look better than it really is.
- **Tiny example:** No-profile GPT-5 is uncannily well-calibrated on approval (24.5%) but critical only 11.3% — about a third of the real rate. With-profile Gemini approves 72.2% (3× real) and is critical just 3.7%. Only the dumb `prior_sampler` (35.2%) reaches the real critical rate.
- **Gotcha:** The two numbers can flatter independently — GPT-5's approval looks perfect at 24.5% while its criticism is still far too low. Always read **both** together, and against the real 24% / 33%, not in isolation.

---

**Card 5 — Easy mode**
- **Plain name:** The too-polite problem
- **One-liner:** When a simulator is too agreeable — approving too much and rarely pushing back — so the coding agent it tests looks better than real users would ever let it.
- **Why it matters:** The whole point of a user simulator is to stress-test a coding agent the way a real, sometimes-annoyed developer would. A too-polite simulator removes the friction — the bug reports, interruptions, pushback — and quietly grades the agent on a curve. An agent that passes against an easy-mode simulator may still frustrate real developers. This is the headline failure of every live model tested.
- **Tiny example:** Every live model approves more than the real 24% (39% to 72%) and is critical far less than the real 33% (only

- **Plain name:** Right-mix score — does the simulator have the right move-blend?
- **One-liner:** How closely the simulator's overall blend of move-types (approve, push back, report bugs, ask questions…) matches the blend real developers produce. 100 = identical blend.
- **Why it matters:** If the simulator never reports bugs or never pushes back, the coding agent only ever gets tested on easy days. MoveFid checks the diet: across many turns, does the simulator approve, redirect, and complain in roughly the same proportions as real people?
- **Tiny example:** Real developers approve 24%, do new work 20%, refine/redirect 15%, report bugs 13%, push back 11%, interrupt 9%, ask questions 6%. A simulator that hits those same percentages scores high.
- **Gotcha:** MoveFid only looks at the *overall* blend, not whether each move lands at the right moment — so it's gameable. The brainless `prior_sampler`, which literally draws moves from the real mix, **tops the chart at 84.3** with zero situational skill. A high MoveFid alone tells you nothing about whether the simulator pushes back when the code is actually broken.

---

**Card 2 — CondAgree**
- **Plain name:** Right-move-right-place score
- **One-liner:** Of the 54 real moments, the fraction where the simulator made the very same move the real developer made at that exact moment. (Scale 0–1.)
- **Why it matters:** Matching the average blend is cheap; reacting correctly turn-by-turn is the real test. CondAgree asks the harder question — at this specific point in this specific conversation, did the simulator do what the real human did?
- **Tiny example:** Gemini-3.1-Pro with a developer profile scores 0.333 — it picked the real developer's exact move at about a third of the 54 moments. DeepSeek-V3.1 with-profile scores 0.315; the dumb `prior_sampler` scores only 0.185.
- **Gotcha:** A third sounds low, but moves are a 7-way choice, so the bar isn't 50% — it's the lucky-guess line of 0.163 (next card). And scoring well here doesn't mean the simulator is well-behaved: Gemini has the highest CondAgree yet approves 72% of the time — right-move-right-place *and* badly easy-mode at once. (With 54 moments and one run per cell, "highest" means leading, not provably best.)

---

**Card 3 — The lucky-guess line (0.163)**
- **Plain name:** The lucky-guess line *(formula name, small/muted: marginal ceiling, Σp²)*
- **One-liner:** The CondAgree you'd get by blindly guessing moves from the real mix without understanding the moment at all — so anything clearly above it means real situational skill.
- **Why it matters:** Without a reference point, a CondAgree number is meaningless. This is that reference: the score you get purely by luck if you know the right average proportions but read nothing about what's happening. Beating 0.163 is the proof a simulator is actually reading the situation.
- **Tiny example:** Guess by the real mix and you'll say "approve" about 24% of the time, while the real dev also approved about 24% — so you accidentally agree on approve about 0.24 × 0.24 of the time. Add up that lucky overlap across all seven moves and it comes to 0.163. The dumb `prior_sampler` scores 0.185 — sitting on the line. The with-profile frontier runs hit 0.278–0.333, about 1.7× to 2× the line.
- **Gotcha:** Clearing the line by a hair is noise, not skill (only 54 moments, one run per cell). And a simulator can beat the line on situational accuracy while still being badly miscalibrated on how often it approves or complains — the line says "has real skill," not "behaves like a real developer." (Even a constant "always approve" baseline clears it, at 0.241.)

---

**Card 4 — approve% and critical%**
- **Plain name:** The temperament check — how nice vs. how tough?
- **One-liner:** The simulator's own rate of saying "looks good, continue" (approve%) versus its rate of saying something's wrong — pushback + interrupt + bug report (critical%) — measured against real developers' **24%** and **33%**.
- **Why it matters:** This is the quickest read on whether a simulator behaves like a real developer or like a pushover. Real developers approve about 1 turn in 4 and object about 1 turn in 3. A simulator that approves far more and objects far less makes any coding agent look better than it really is.
- **Tiny example:** No-profile GPT-5 is uncannily well-calibrated on approval (24.5%) but critical only 11.3% — about a third of the real rate. With-profile Gemini approves 72.2% (3× real) and is critical just 3.7%. Only the dumb `prior_sampler` (35.2%) reaches the real critical rate.
- **Gotcha:** The two numbers can flatter independently — GPT-5's approval looks perfect at 24.5% while its criticism is still far too low. Always read **both** together, and against the real 24% / 33%, not in isolation.

---

**Card 5 — Easy mode**
- **Plain name:** The too-polite problem
- **One-liner:** When a simulator is too agreeable — approving too much and rarely pushing back — so the coding agent it tests looks better than real users would ever let it.
- **Why it matters:** The whole point of a user simulator is to stress-test a coding agent the way a real, sometimes-annoyed developer would. A too-polite simulator removes the friction — the bug reports, interruptions, pushback — and quietly grades the agent on a curve. An agent that passes against an easy-mode simulator may still frustrate real developers. This is the headline failure of every live model tested.
- **Tiny example:** Every live model approves more than the real 24% (39% to 72%) and is critical far less than the real 33% (only

content.content:
## 4. LEADERBOARD TABLE

**Columns (left → right):**
1. Simulator
2. Mode (`with-profile` / `no-profile` / `reference`)
3. MoveFid `(0–100, higher = mix closer to real)`
4. CondAgree `(of 54 moments; lucky-guess line = 0.163)`
5. approve% `(real 24%)`
6. critical% `(real 33%)`

**Live models (render first, blue/neutral):**

| Simulator | Mode | MoveFid | CondAgree | approve% | critical% |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v3.1 | with-profile | 53.9 | 0.315 | 42.6% | 9.3% |
| deepseek-v3.1 | no-profile | 39.8 | 0.189 | 49.1% | 5.7% |
| gpt-5 | with-profile | 45.5 | 0.278 | 38.9% | 3.7% |
| gpt-5 | no-profile | 52.4 | 0.189 | 24.5% | 11.3% |
| gemini-3.1-pro | with-profile | 41.4 | 0.333 | 72.2% | 3.7% |
| gemini-3.1-pro | no-profile | 49.0 | 0.278 | 66.7% | 11.1% |

**— References — (render in a visually demoted bottom group: zinc-100 background, divider, "References" label):**

| Simulator | Mode | MoveFid | CondAgree | approve% | critical% |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| prior_sampler | reference | 84.3 * | 0.185 | 20.4% | 35.2% |
| always_approve | reference | 4.9 | 0.241 | 100% | 0% |

**Cell-treatment notes for the engineer:**
- `prior_sampler` MoveFid `84.3 *` carries an amber asterisk → tooltip/footnote: "Tops MoveFid, but has no situational skill — see the lucky-guess line."
- CondAgree column: draw a thin amber tick at **0.163** inside each cell on a 0–0.40 scale; emerald dot if clearly above, amber dot if at/just-above. Note that no-profile deepseek (0.189), no-profile gpt-5 (0.189), and `prior_sampler` (0.185) all sit *barely* above the line — render them visually similar so no false separation is implied.
- approve% text colored emerald only when within ~5 pts of real 24% (only no-profile gpt-5, 24.5%); otherwise zinc.

**Plain-English note under the table:**
> Read every row against the real **24% approve / 33% critical**. **critical% = pushback + interrupt + bug report**, and all percentages are out of **54 moments** (so 11% ≈ 6 turns). No single column names a winner: the highest CondAgree is with-profile Gemini (0.333) — genuine situational skill, ≈2× the 0.163 lucky-guess line — yet it approves 72.2%. The highest MoveFid is the dumb `prior_sampler` (84.3), which has no situational skill at all. And note the live models top out around MoveFid 54, so ~50 here is middling, not strong. With one generation per cell, treat small gaps between rows as noise; the easy-mode pattern (everyone above 24% approve, everyone below 33% critical) is the robust signal.

---

## 5. VISUALS

Light theme. Color semantics site-wide: **emerald** = at/near real or clearly above the lucky-guess line (good); **amber** = easy-mode / threshold / caution; **blue** = live frontier model (neutral); **zinc** = reference rows + tracks. Mono font for every numeral. All visuals are plain `div`/SVG bars — no chart library.

**Section render order (top → bottom):**
1. Hero stat + mini-bar *(spec in §1)*
2. Metric primer cards *(spec in §3)*
3. Move-mix comparison: Real vs. simulators (centerpiece)
4. Leaderboard table *(spec in §4)*
5. The dumb-sampler trap (MoveFid × CondAgree scatter)
6. The persona-profile lift (with-profile vs. no-profile dumbbells)
7. The transfer story (3 model callouts)
8. Caveats footer *(spec in §6)*

---

**VISUAL A — Move-mix comparison: Real vs. simulators (centerpiece)**

Two stacked sub-panels: **APPROVE%** and **CRITICAL%**. Each panel has 8 horizontal bars (6 live runs + 2 references), scale 0–100%, with a dashed emerald vertical baseline.

*APPROVE% panel — baseline dashed line at 24% ("real 24%"):*

| Row | Value | Bar color |
|---|---|---|
| deepseek · with-profile | 42.6% | blue |
| deepseek · no-profile | 49.1% | blue |
| gpt-5 · with-profile | 38.9% | blue |
| gpt-5 · no-profile | 24.5% | emerald (≈ baseline; flag "best-calibrated, no-profile only") |
| gemini · with-profile | 72.2% | amber (worst offender) |
| gemini · no-profile | 66.7% | amber |
| [ref] prior_sampler | 20.4% | zinc |
| [ref] always_approve | 100% | zinc (pinned far right) |

*CRITICAL% panel — baseline dashed line at 33% ("real 33%"):*

| Row | Value | Bar color |
|---|---|---|
| deepseek · with-profile | 9.3% | amber |
| deepseek · no-profile | 5.7% | amber |
| gpt-5 · with-profile | 3.7% | amber |
| gpt-5 · no-profile | 11.3% | amber (tallest live model) |
| gemini · with-profile | 3.7% | amber |
| gemini · no-profile | 11.1% | amber |
| [ref] prior_sampler | 35.2% | zinc (only row reaching the baseline) |
| [ref] always_approve | 0% | zinc (empty bar) |

**Caption:** Read against the dashed real-dev lines. On approve%, every live model sits *at or past* 24% — Gemini nearly triples it. On critical%, every live model falls *short* of 33%, clustering at 3.7–11.3%. Only the dumb `prior_sampler` reaches the real critical rate (35.2%), and only `always_approve` hits 100% approve. The simulators are too polite.

---

**VISUAL B — The dumb-sampler trap (MoveFid × CondAgree scatter)**

One `<svg viewBox="0 0 100 100">`. **X = MoveFid (0–100)**, **Y = CondAgree (0–0.40)**, mapped `cy = 100 − CondAgree/0.40*100`. Horizontal dashed amber line across the plot at the 0.163 y-position, labeled `lucky-guess = 0.163`. Faint emerald band above it labeled "real situational skill."

*Points (x = MoveFid, y = CondAgree):*
- deepseek · with-profile (53.9, 0.315) — blue
- deepseek · no-profile (39.8, 0.189) — blue, just above line
- gpt-5 · with-profile (45.5, 0.278) — blue
- gpt-5 · no-profile (52.4, 0.189) — blue, just above line
- gemini · with-profile (41.4, 0.333) — blue, highest dot; label "≈2× the line"
- gemini · no-profile (49.0, 0.278) — blue
- **prior_sampler (84.3, 0.185)** — zinc, far right, on the amber line; label "tops MoveFid, no skill"
- always_approve (4.9, 0.241) — zinc, far left

**Caption:** `prior_sampler` just draws moves from the real mix — so it *wins* MoveFid (84.3, far right) but lands on the lucky-guess line (0.185 ≈ 0.163): no idea what's happening at any given moment. The real models score lower MoveFid yet sit above the line — they read the moment. High MoveFid is gameable; the pair is not. (Even `always_approve` clears the line, at 0.241 — so the line is a floor, not a finish line.)

---

**VISUAL C — The persona-profile lift (dumbbells)**

Three slim dumbbell rows, one per model, on a 0–0.40 CondAgree axis. Hollow dot = no-profile, filled dot = with-profile, connector line between, `+delta` labeled trailing. A small amber sub-note per row shows the approve% side-effect.

*CondAgree (no-profile → with-profile, delta):*
- deepseek-v3.1: 0.189 → 0.315, **+0.126** (emerald; biggest gain — "most coachable")
- gpt-5: 0.189 → 0.278, **+0.089** (emerald)
- gemini-3.1-pro: 0.278 → 0.333, **+0.055** (emerald)

*approve% side-effect (no-profile → with-profile), amber sub-note:*
- deepseek: 49.1% → 42.6% (down — improves)
- gpt-5: 24.5% → 38.9% (**up** — approves more; amber)
- gemini: 66.7% → 72.2% (**up** — approves more; amber)

**Caption:** Giving the simulator a profile of the specific developer raises right-move-right-place for all three (DeepSeek gains most, +0.126). But it doesn't cure easy mode: for GPT-5 and Gemini the profile makes them approve *even more* (24.5→38.9%, 66.7→72.2%). With one run per cell, read the direction, not the exact ordering of the deltas.

---

**VISUAL D — The transfer story (3 callout cards)**

Three equal text-forward cards, each: prior-work chip → what happened here, with one highlighted numeral.

- **DeepSeek-V3.1** — chip `prior work: best simulator (USI 76.0)`. Here: "the most **coachable** — biggest profile gain (**+0.126**) and top with-profile MoveFid (**53.9**)." Tone: emerald.
- **GPT-5** — chip `prior work: strong, not best (USI ~70.9)`. Here: "best-calibrated approve rate with no profile (**24.5%** ≈ real 24%) — yet still barely pushes back (critical **11.3%**)." Tone: blue/amber.
- **Gemini-3.1-Pro** — chip `prior work: most human-like`. Here: "the **worst** easy-mode offender — approves **72.2%**, ~3× real. Sounding human ≠ behaving like a developer." Tone: amber.

**Caption (full-width below):** USI was the combined quality score from prior customer-service-simulator research. Today's top models are too polite to stand in for real developers. Being good at *sounding human*, or at *simulating customers*, does not make a good *coder* simulator.

---

## 6. CAVEATS

Render as one muted line (zinc-500, small) in the footer, and echo a one-line version beside the leaderboard ("One generation per cell — treat small row-to-row gaps as noise; the easy-mode pattern is the robust signal.").

> **Caveats.** This rests on 54 moments from 9 users, with one generation per cell (so no error bars — treat tiny gaps as noise), moves labeled by a single AI classifier (claude-haiku-4.5, whose errors bound every number), percentages that exclude the small "other" move category and so may not sum to exactly 100%, and a **request-side** setup — we held the real coding agent fixed and asked for the developer's next message, rather than letting the simulator drive a live agent and measuring task outcomes. The easy-mode effect, though, is large and consistent across every live model.

ls -la "$SHOT"
